match.question_text: A controller encounters a traffic situation that is not addressed anywhere in FAA Order JO 7110.65. What is the controller required to do?

replacements.question_text: A controller encounters a traffic situation that is not addressed anywhere in FAA Order JO 7110.65. What is the controller required to do?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-1, controllers must exercise their best judgment when encountering situations not covered by the order. They are not required to stop providing service or wait for a supervisor to draft guidance.

choices.text: Refuse to provide any further ATC service until a supervisor creates a written procedure.
Apply the most similar procedure in the order even if it contradicts the actual situation.
Continue providing service using best judgment to handle the situation safely.
Delegate all decision-making to the pilot-in-command for that flight only.

replacements.question_text: What does FAA Order JO 7110.65 prescribe for persons providing air traffic control services?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-1, the order prescribes both air traffic control procedures and phraseology. Knowing it prescribes both (not just one) is foundational to understanding the order's scope.

choices.text: Air traffic control procedures and phraseology.
Only air traffic control procedures.
Only standard ATC phraseology.
Staffing minimums and equipment maintenance schedules.

match.question_text: An ARTCC and an adjacent TRACON develop a new procedure for arrival handoffs that requires both facilities to coordinate. Does this procedure need to be documented in a Letter of Agreement (LOA)?

replacements.question_text: An ARTCC and an adjacent TRACON develop a new procedure for arrival handoffs that requires both facilities to coordinate. Does this procedure need to be documented in a Letter of Agreement (LOA)?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-10, procedures or minima applied jointly or requiring cooperation or concurrence of more than one facility or organization must be documented in an LOA. Multi-facility coordination triggers the LOA requirement.

choices.text: No, because LOAs are only required for military-civilian coordination.
No, because each facility can document the procedure in its own internal directives.
Yes, but only if the procedure involves reduced separation minima.
Yes, because the procedure is applied jointly and requires cooperation between more than one facility.

match.question_text: How do Letters of Agreement (LOAs) relate to FAA Order JO 7110.65?

replacements.question_text: How do Letters of Agreement (LOAs) relate to FAA Order JO 7110.65?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order — they do not replace or override it. Their minima may not be less than the order's unless appropriate military authority authorizes reduced separation between military aircraft.

choices.text: LOAs replace the order for the facilities that sign them.
LOAs only supplement the order and cannot specify lower minima except when military authority authorizes reduced separation between military aircraft.
LOAs may set any minima the signing facilities agree to, even if lower than the order.
LOAs are advisory only and create no binding procedural obligations.

match.question_text: Two facilities want to document joint procedures in a Letter of Agreement (LOA). Which statement correctly describes what an LOA may do regarding separation minima?

replacements.question_text: Two facilities want to document joint procedures in a Letter of Agreement (LOA). Which statement correctly describes what an LOA may do regarding separation minima?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs may not specify minima less than the order's minima unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft. Civilian facilities cannot self-authorize lower minima through an LOA.

choices.text: An LOA may not reduce minima below the order unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft.
An LOA may reduce minima below the order whenever both facilities agree the reduction is safe.
An LOA may reduce minima below the order for any aircraft type if the reduction is published in a NOTAM.
An LOA may always set lower minima because it is a binding agreement between facilities.

match.question_text: A facility is processing a waiver request that involves military operations. The facility supervisor obtains FAA regional approval first, planning to notify the military headquarters afterward. Is this the correct sequence?

replacements.question_text: A facility is processing a waiver request that involves military operations. The facility supervisor obtains FAA regional approval first, planning to notify the military headquarters afterward. Is this the correct sequence?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-11b, prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters is required for subsequent interface with FAA. Military approval must come first, not after FAA coordination.

choices.text: Yes, because FAA coordination must precede military notification in all cases.
No, because waivers involving military operations require prior military headquarters approval before FAA processes the waiver.
Yes, because the military notification is only for record-keeping after the waiver is approved.
No, because waivers with military involvement are prohibited entirely under any circumstances.

replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-12, all ATO employees must strive to continuously improve the ATO SMS. SMS ownership is assigned to all employees at all levels, not just safety specialists.

choices.text: Submit one SMS suggestion per calendar year at minimum.
Complete SMS training only if designated as an SMS practitioner.
Report safety data only to their immediate supervisor.

replacements.question_text: A facility is physically located in the Eastern region but its service area office is in the Central region. The facility needs to contact a non-ATO FAA regional office. Which region is correct?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the facility contacts the region where it is physically located — not the service area office region. Physical location governs over organizational alignment for these non-ATO contacts.

choices.text: The Central region, because that is where the service area office is located.
The Eastern region, because the facility is physically located there.
Either region, at the facility's discretion.
Neither; the facility must contact Washington headquarters directly.

replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-14b, all organizations are responsible for viewing, downloading, and subscribing to receive email notifications when changes occur. Passive awareness is insufficient — organizations must actively monitor and obtain changes.

choices.text: Wait for a printed copy to arrive by mail before taking any action.
View, download, and subscribe to receive email notifications of changes.
Only download changes when a supervisor specifically directs it.

replacements.question_text: A contractor performing work for the ATO relies on FAA Order JO 7110.65 directives but claims the order does not apply because they are not an FAA employee. Is the contractor correct?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-2, the order applies to all ATO personnel AND anyone using ATO directives. The second category ('anyone using ATO directives') captures contractors, military personnel, and others regardless of employment relationship.

choices.text: Yes; the order applies only to direct FAA employees with an active ATC certification.
Yes, but only if the contract does not explicitly reference the order.
No; the order applies to anyone using ATO directives, including contractors.

The order is only distributed in printed form to facility managers.
The order is available on public FAA websites accessible without an FAA login.

replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-4, JO 7110.65AA and all changes to it are canceled when JO 7110.65BB is issued. The prior edition and its changes do not survive independently — controllers must use the current edition.

choices.text: JO 7110.65AA changes remain in effect independently until individually reviewed.
JO 7110.65AA continues in effect alongside JO 7110.65BB until the next AIRAC date.

match.question_text: A new change to FAA Order JO 7110.65 is published. A trainee asks whether the Explanation of Changes pages from the original basic order should be kept or discarded now that Change 1 has arrived. What is the correct guidance?

replacements.question_text: A new change to FAA Order JO 7110.65 is published. A trainee asks whether the Explanation of Changes pages from the original basic order should be kept or discarded now that Change 1 has arrived. What is the correct guidance?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-5, it is advisable to retain the Explanation of Changes pages throughout the duration of the basic order — not just until the next change arrives. The pages document the full change history for the basic order's life.

choices.text: Retain the Explanation of Changes pages throughout the duration of the basic order.
Discard the original Explanation of Changes pages; only the most recent change's pages matter.
Each facility decides independently how long to retain them.
Keep them only if the change affects your specific facility type.

match.question_text: In the FAA Order JO 7110.65 publication schedule, what does the 'Cutoff Date for Completion' represent?

replacements.question_text: In the FAA Order JO 7110.65 publication schedule, what does the 'Cutoff Date for Completion' represent?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-6b, the Cutoff Date for Completion is the deadline for a proposed change to be fully coordinated and signed. Change initiators must submit well in advance of this date because the review and coordination process often takes several months.

choices.text: The deadline for a proposed change to be fully coordinated and signed.
The date by which facilities must have printed and distributed the change.
The date after which no new changes may be proposed for the current edition.
The date controllers must complete training on the new procedures.

match.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 specifies a common time threshold for three activities: website availability before the effective date, FAA facility notification of non-receipt, and military facility notification of non-receipt. What is this threshold?

replacements.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 specifies a common time threshold for three activities: website availability before the effective date, FAA facility notification of non-receipt, and military facility notification of non-receipt. What is this threshold?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-7, all three provisions use the same 30-day threshold before the effective date: the order is available on the FAA website 30 days prior, FAA facilities notify the service area distribution officer if not received 30 days prior, and military facilities notify their headquarters if not received 30 days prior.

choices.text: 14 days before the effective date.
60 days before the effective date.
30 days before the effective date.
45 days before the effective date.

replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-8a, personnel should submit recommended changes in procedures to facility management. From there, the proposal follows the formal submission chain through appropriate channels to AJV-P.

choices.text: Directly to AJV-P at 9-AJV-P-HQ-Correspondence@faa.gov.

match.question_text: A proposed procedural change for FAA Order JO 7110.65 has been fully coordinated, signed, and approved. However, the operational system software has not yet been updated to support the new procedure. Can the change be published in the order?

replacements.question_text: A proposed procedural change for FAA Order JO 7110.65 has been fully coordinated, signed, and approved. However, the operational system software has not yet been updated to support the new procedure. Can the change be published in the order?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-8d, procedural changes will not be made to the order until the operational system software has been adapted to accomplish the revised procedures. Software readiness is a prerequisite, not an afterthought.

choices.text: Yes; once coordinated and signed, the change can be published immediately.
Yes, but only if a waiver under the waiver process has also been approved.
Yes; software adaptation is advisory only and does not block publication.
No; procedural changes are not made to the order until the operational system software has been adapted.

match.question_text: A DoD controller and a non-FAA industry representative both want to request an interpretation of FAA Order JO 7110.65. How do their routing paths differ?

replacements.question_text: A DoD controller and a non-FAA industry representative both want to request an interpretation of FAA Order JO 7110.65. How do their routing paths differ?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-9d and 1-1-9e, DoD personnel must route requests through the chain of command to the respective Military Service Headquarters first; non-FAA/DoD requests may be submitted directly to AJV-P. The chain-of-command requirement applies only to DoD personnel.

choices.text: Both may submit directly to AJV-P without any intermediate review.
DoD requests go directly to AJV-P; non-FAA/DoD must route through the chain of command.
Both must go through the chain of command before reaching AJV-P.
DoD must route through chain of command to Military Service Headquarters first; non-FAA/DoD may go directly to AJV-P.

replacements.question_text: An AJT field request for interpretation of FAA Order JO 7110.65 arrives at the Service Area Director's office. What determination must the Service Area Director make?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-1-9a2, the Service Area Director must determine whether an interpretation or a clarification is required. Interpretation requests go to AJV-P; clarification requests are returned to the OSG for a written response.

choices.text: Whether the order paragraph has been superseded by a waiver.
Whether the request should be forwarded to the Military Service Headquarters.
Whether the request originates from an FAA or a military facility.
Whether an interpretation or a clarification is required.

match.question_text: A controller checks the time at 14:35:42 UTC. Under FAA Order JO 7110.65 conventions, what time should be recorded for ATC operational purposes, and how would a time check be given?

replacements.question_text: A controller checks the time at 14:35:42 UTC. Under FAA Order JO 7110.65 conventions, what time should be recorded for ATC operational purposes, and how would a time check be given?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-1m, standard ATC operational time changes at the minute + 30 seconds (so 14:35:42 records as 14:35). Time checks are given to the nearest quarter minute (14:35:42 would be given as 14:35 and three-quarters). Both use UTC.

choices.text: Record 14:36 for operations; time check given as 1435 and three-quarters.
Record 14:35 for operations; time check given to the nearest quarter minute.
Record 14:36 for operations; time check given to the nearest whole minute.
Record 14:35 for operations; time check given in local time, not UTC.

match.question_text: A paragraph in FAA Order JO 7110.65 states that visibility must be at least 3 miles. Another paragraph requires 5 miles of separation. In each case, what kind of miles are referenced by default?

replacements.question_text: A paragraph in FAA Order JO 7110.65 states that visibility must be at least 3 miles. Another paragraph requires 5 miles of separation. In each case, what kind of miles are referenced by default?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-1k, 'miles' means nautical miles unless otherwise specified, and means statute miles in conjunction with visibility. So visibility uses statute miles; separation uses nautical miles.

choices.text: Visibility uses statute miles; separation uses nautical miles.
Both use nautical miles by default.
Both use statute miles by default.
It depends on whether the facility is domestic or oceanic.

replacements.question_text: A procedure in FAA Order JO 7110.65 references 'the runway.' The facility has both a concrete runway and a helipad with a takeoff course. Does the procedure apply to the helipad unless otherwise specified?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-1n, 'runway' does not include helipads and/or their accompanying takeoff/landing courses unless otherwise specified. The default definition excludes helipads.

choices.text: Yes; helipads are a subset of runways and are always included.
Yes, but only if the helipad has a paved surface.
It depends on whether the helipad is co-located on the same physical surface as the runway.
No; 'runway' does not include helipads or their takeoff/landing courses unless otherwise specified.

match.question_text: For ATC operational timekeeping, when does the change to the next minute occur?

replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-1m, the change to the next minute is made at the minute plus 30 seconds. For example, 1435:30 becomes 1436. Time checks are an exception — they are given to the nearest quarter minute.

choices.text: At exactly the top of the minute (minute + 00 seconds).
At the minute plus 15 seconds.

Any separation the controller believes is safe under the circumstances.

match.question_text: Two aircraft have intersecting protected airspaces with an angular difference of exactly 135 degrees. How are their courses classified under FAA Order JO 7110.65?

replacements.question_text: Two aircraft have intersecting protected airspaces with an angular difference of exactly 135 degrees. How are their courses classified under FAA Order JO 7110.65?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-2b, crossing courses include 45 through 135 degrees inclusive. The opposite/reciprocal range begins above 135 degrees (greater than 135). So exactly 135 degrees is classified as crossing.

choices.text: Opposite/reciprocal courses, because 135 marks the start of that range.
Crossing courses, because crossing includes 45 through 135 degrees inclusive.
Same courses, because the angle is less than 180 degrees.
The classification is ambiguous because 135 is a boundary value.

replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-2a, same courses have an angular difference of less than 45 degrees. Same courses also require that protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect — angular difference alone is insufficient.

choices.text: Less than 45 degrees.
Less than 30 degrees.
Less than 90 degrees.

match.question_text: An en route controller reads a procedure annotated TERMINAL in FAA Order JO 7110.65. Does the annotated procedure apply to the en route controller?

replacements.question_text: An en route controller reads a procedure annotated TERMINAL in FAA Order JO 7110.65. Does the annotated procedure apply to the en route controller?
replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-5d, paragraphs annotated with EN ROUTE, OCEANIC, or TERMINAL apply only to the designated type facility. The TERMINAL annotation means en route controllers do not apply that paragraph. Without any such annotation, paragraphs apply to all facility types.

choices.text: Yes, but only if the en route facility has a terminal radar function.
No; TERMINAL annotation means only terminal facilities apply that paragraph.
Yes; facility-type annotations are advisory, not restrictive.
No, but the en route controller may apply it if the terminal facility concurs.

replacements.explanation: Per 1-2-5c, bold lines adjacent to body text indicate individual substantive revisions at that location. Bold lines adjacent to a title mean extensive changes have been made to that entire chapter, section, or paragraph — a much broader scope of revision.

match.question_text: How does the controller's role differ when a pilot has declared an emergency versus when the controller believes an alternative action would be more beneficial?

replacements.question_text: How does the controller's role differ when a pilot has declared an emergency versus when the controller believes an alternative action would be more beneficial?
replacements.explanation: Per 10-1-2a, assistance must be consistent with the pilot's requests, but if the controller believes an alternative course may prove more beneficial, the controller transmits the recommendation to the pilot rather than issuing a mandatory instruction. The pilot retains PIC authority under 14 CFR § 91.3.

choices.text: The controller must override the pilot's request and issue the alternative as a mandatory clearance.
The controller provides assistance consistent with the pilot's requests but transmits any alternative recommendation rather than imposing it.

replacements.question_text: Why does 10-2-18 prohibit climb clearances specifically for turbine-powered aircraft (not piston aircraft) during volcanic ash encounters?
replacements.explanation: The distinction is tied to the engine type. Turbine engines ingest enormous volumes of air and the ash melts in the combustion chamber, coating turbine blades and causing power loss. Piston engines are less susceptible because their intake and combustion processes differ. The note confirms high-bypass turbofan engines (a type of turbine) have experienced total power loss from ash ingestion, and damage increases as engine power increases.

choices.text: Piston aircraft are exempt because they operate at lower altitudes where volcanic ash is less concentrated.
Turbine engines ingest high volumes of air; ash melts in the combustion chamber and coats turbine blades, causing power loss that worsens with increased power settings.
Turbine aircraft are larger and carry more passengers, so the prohibition is a passenger-safety measure, not an engineering one.
Piston engines are not affected by volcanic ash at all because their air intake filtering is superior.

match.question_text: A controller has an emergency aircraft on a working frequency with good communications. Which is a valid reason to change frequencies?

replacements.question_text: A controller has an emergency aircraft on a working frequency with good communications. Which is a valid reason to change frequencies?
replacements.explanation: 10-2-2 permits a frequency change only when there is a valid reason. Simply being on a non-emergency frequency is NOT a valid reason — the rule explicitly says it might be best to keep the aircraft on the initial contact frequency even though 121.5 and 243.0 are emergency frequencies. A handoff to a better-equipped facility would be a valid reason.

choices.text: The aircraft is not on 121.5 or 243.0, which are the designated emergency frequencies.
A supervisor wants all emergencies consolidated on guard frequency.
The aircraft must be handed off to a facility better positioned to provide assistance.
The controller's sector is about to go on break and someone else should handle it.

Immediately to 121.5 MHz since that is the designated emergency frequency

Whenever the pilot requests any frequency change, regardless of reason

match.question_text: How does 'pilotage' differ from 'sighting by other aircraft' as an orientation method?

replacements.question_text: How does 'pilotage' differ from 'sighting by other aircraft' as an orientation method?
replacements.explanation: Pilotage is navigation by reference to visible landmarks by the pilot of the distressed aircraft. Sighting by other aircraft involves a second aircraft visually locating the distressed aircraft and reporting its position. They are distinct methods — one relies on the distressed pilot's own visual references and the other relies on an external aircraft's observation.

choices.text: Pilotage uses the distressed pilot's own landmark references while sighting by other aircraft uses a second aircraft to locate and report the distressed aircraft's position.
They are the same method described with different terminology for en route versus terminal environments.
Pilotage is radar-based while sighting by other aircraft is visual-only.
Sighting by other aircraft requires NAVAID confirmation; pilotage does not.

replacements.question_text: Altitude-change guidance for emergency aircraft includes a procedural note warning that volcanic ash ingestion damage to high-bypass turbofan engines increases as engine power increases. Which controller recommendation does this note most directly constrain?
replacements.explanation: The note explains that volcanic ash ingestion damage increases as engine power is increased, so climb while in the ash cloud is to be avoided where terrain permits. This directly constrains the recommendation in the body of 10-2-4 to increase altitude — if the aircraft is in volcanic ash, that recommendation should NOT be made. The note operates as an exception/exclusion to the primary rule.

choices.text: The recommendation to maintain altitude, because staying level increases ash exposure duration.
The recommendation to increase altitude, because climbing increases engine power and therefore ash damage.
The entire paragraph, because communications and radar are irrelevant during ash encounters.
It does not constrain the paragraph — it is purely informational background not affecting controller actions.

Either radar contact OR radio communications loss alone triggers emergency consideration.
Radar contact loss triggers it immediately; radio loss is a separate notification requirement.
Only the radar contact loss matters; radio communications are assumed lost whenever radar is lost.

match.question_text: What distinguishes subparagraph 'a' from subparagraphs 'b' through 'h'?

replacements.question_text: What distinguishes subparagraph 'a' from subparagraphs 'b' through 'h'?
replacements.explanation: Subparagraph a lists situations where an emergency is formally 'declared' (by pilot, facility personnel, officials, or system-generated transmission). Subparagraphs b through h list circumstances where an emergency is to be considered even without a formal declaration — unexpected loss of contact, forced landing indicators, crew abandonment, emergency codes, intercept/escort needs, likely ground rescue, and ELT signals. The distinction is that a triggers on a declaration while b-h trigger on observed or reported conditions.

choices.text: Subparagraph a applies only to IFR aircraft; b through h apply to VFR aircraft.
Subparagraph a requires notifying the RCC; b through h require notifying only the ARTCC.
There is no substantive distinction — all eight are equivalent emergency triggers.
Subparagraph a lists formal emergency declarations; subparagraphs b through h list conditions that indicate an emergency even without a declaration.

match.question_text: How does the procedure differ when a pilot verbally declares a hijacking versus when the pilot squawks 7500 without verbal communication, ?

replacements.question_text: How does the procedure differ when a pilot verbally declares a hijacking versus when the pilot squawks 7500 without verbal communication, ?
replacements.explanation: 10-2-6a handles verbal notification: assign code 7500, and if the pilot acknowledges the code without further communication or fails to acknowledge/communicate further, assume hijack. 10-2-6b handles squawk-only notification: verify the squawk with 'VERIFY SQUAWKING SEVEN FIVE ZERO ZERO,' and if the pilot fails to acknowledge or communicate further, assume hijack. In both cases: no reference to hijacking in subsequent communications unless initiated by the pilot or directed by the DEN ATSC, and immediately inform the operations manager, supervisor, or CIC.

choices.text: Verbal notification is treated as a higher threat level requiring immediate military escort; squawk-only is treated as precautionary.
There is no procedural difference — in both cases the controller assigns code 7500 using the same phraseology.
Verbal notification requires supervisor approval before assigning 7500; squawk-only does not.

Verbal notification requires assigning 7500; squawk-only notification requires verifying the code selection. Both result in the same restrictions on further communication and supervisory notification.

match.question_text: Turns should be made while in VFR conditions so that the aircraft flies straight in IFR conditions. What is the operational safety rationale?

replacements.question_text: Turns should be made while in VFR conditions so that the aircraft flies straight in IFR conditions. What is the operational safety rationale?
replacements.explanation: A non-instrument-rated pilot is susceptible to spatial disorientation when turning without visual references. By completing turns in VFR conditions, the aircraft enters IFR conditions already established on a straight course, reducing the risk of the pilot losing control during the transition to instrument-only reference.

choices.text: Completing turns in visual conditions avoids spatial disorientation risk for a pilot who lacks instrument training when entering clouds.
It is more fuel efficient to turn in VFR because the aircraft can use visual checkpoints for navigation.
ATC radar tracking is more accurate when aircraft turn in VFR conditions.
It simplifies the controller's workload because VFR turns require fewer radio transmissions to coordinate.

replacements.question_text: A sector team has been too busy to update an aircraft's interim altitude in the automation. One controller argues the entry can be skipped because an exception in the automation rules allows this when workload is high. Is the controller correct?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-10(b) provides an exception to the interim altitude entry requirement only for certain ARTCC sectors, and only if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive. Workload is not a basis for the exception. Unless a facility directive specifically authorizes the exception for that sector, the entry requirement remains in effect regardless of how busy the sector is.

choices.text: Yes. The exception applies whenever the sector team determines that the entry would interfere with higher-priority duties.
Yes, but only if the aircraft is level at the new altitude as confirmed by Mode C for at least two radar updates.
No. The exception applies only to certain ARTCC sectors, and only if explicitly defined in a facility directive. Workload alone does not authorize skipping the entry.
No. There are no exceptions to the requirement to enter interim altitudes under any circumstances.

replacements.explanation: 13-1-10 Note 1 states that if trajectory-modeling data is not current, the aircraft entries and notification of probe results for surrounding sectors and facilities, as well as the subject sector, may be misleading. The impact of stale data extends beyond the controller's own sector.

choices.text: The automation automatically deletes the flight plan and requires re-filing.
Only the aircraft list display is affected; the conflict probe continues unaffected using radar track data.
The EDST disables conflict probe for that aircraft and issues a system alert to the operations supervisor.
Aircraft entries and probe result notifications for surrounding sectors and facilities, as well as the subject sector, may be misleading.

Verbal relay to TMU without any written record, as TMU maintains the official log.

match.question_text: A controller is using the Graphic Plan Display (GPD) to assess whether a potential reroute would keep an aircraft clear of active SUA. Is this an authorized use of the GPD?

replacements.question_text: A controller is using the Graphic Plan Display (GPD) to assess whether a potential reroute would keep an aircraft clear of active SUA. Is this an authorized use of the GPD?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-13(a) authorizes GPD use only to aid in situational awareness and strategic planning. Evaluating whether a reroute would keep an aircraft clear of SUA falls within strategic planning. However, the GPD must not be used for radar identification, position information, transfer of radar identification, radar separation, correlation, or point outs.

choices.text: No, the GPD is only authorized for post-event analysis and may not be used for active traffic planning.
Yes, but only if the operations supervisor specifically authorizes GPD use for each planning action.
No, the GPD is authorized only for pilot briefings and may not be used for controller planning functions.
Yes, the GPD may be used to aid situational awareness and strategic planning, which includes assessing SUA avoidance in advance.

match.question_text: A controller is verifying an aircraft's current altitude. The GPD shows a trajectory-based altitude prediction. The Mode C readout is operational. Which altitude source must the controller use for altitude confirmation?

replacements.question_text: A controller is verifying an aircraft's current altitude. The GPD shows a trajectory-based altitude prediction. The Mode C readout is operational. Which altitude source must the controller use for altitude confirmation?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-13(c) explicitly prohibits using trajectory-based altitude in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation. The GPD trajectory altitude is a prediction based on the flight plan and wind data—it may not reflect the aircraft's actual current altitude. Only Mode C provides actual altitude data for confirmation purposes.

choices.text: Mode C transponder altitude readout, because trajectory-based altitude must not be used as a substitute for Mode C.
The GPD trajectory-based altitude, because it is calculated from the most recent flight plan data and is more current.
Either source, because both Mode C and GPD trajectory altitudes are equally valid for altitude confirmation.
The pilot's verbal report of altitude, because automation data cannot be relied upon for confirmation.

That only trial planning is affected; conflict probe uses radar track data exclusively and is unaffected.

match.question_text: A conflict probe alert shows standard 5 NM separation will be maintained between an A380 and a following B737. The controller knows that greater wake turbulence separation is required behind an A380. What should the controller recognize about this conflict probe alert?

replacements.question_text: A conflict probe alert shows standard 5 NM separation will be maintained between an A380 and a following B737. The controller knows that greater wake turbulence separation is required behind an A380. What should the controller recognize about this conflict probe alert?
replacements.explanation: Per the note to 13-1-2, conflict probe alerts are based on approved radar separation but do not account for instances where greater separation may be needed—such as non-standard formations and A380 operations. The controller must apply additional wake turbulence separation beyond what the conflict probe alert shows. The probe also does not account for situations where reduced separation is permitted (e.g., 3 mile airspace).

choices.text: The alert is definitive and no additional separation is needed, because conflict probe accounts for all aircraft types.
The controller should suppress the alert because it is a false indication for heavy aircraft operations.
The A380 should be reclassified in the system to a standard heavy category to correct the probe calculation.
The alert may not reflect the additional separation needed for A380 wake turbulence, because conflict probe does not account for situations requiring greater separation.

replacements.question_text: Your sector is working two air refueling missions, a formation flight of four F-16s, and three commercial arrivals that need sequencing for the arrival meter. How should these flights be organized on the ACL?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-5(e) says the Special Posting Area (SPA) should be used to group aircraft that have special significance—with air refueling missions, formations, and aircraft to be sequenced listed as examples. Grouping these flights in the SPA helps the sector team maintain awareness of aircraft requiring special attention.

choices.text: Group the refueling missions, formation, and sequencing arrivals in the SPA since they all have special significance.
Leave all flights in the standard ACL. The SPA is reserved only for emergency aircraft.
Move only the formation flight to the SPA. Refueling and sequencing flights do not qualify.
Group them by altitude in the ACL. The SPA function is not relevant to flight organization.

match.question_text: Under 13-1-6(b), after the Coordination Menu is used and the flight plan is subsequently changed, the yellow coding on the Coordination Indicator must be removed:

replacements.question_text: After the Coordination Menu is used and the flight plan is subsequently changed, the yellow coding on the Coordination Indicator must be removed:
replacements.explanation: 13-1-6(b) states the yellow coding is removed 'after any appropriate action has been taken.' It is not removed immediately upon the change, nor is it left indefinitely. The action must be completed first.

choices.text: Immediately when the flight plan change is entered, before any follow-up action is taken.
Only after the aircraft has been handed off and is no longer in the sector.
After any appropriate action has been taken.
Never. The coordination indicator remains as a permanent record of the prior coordination event.

match.question_text: When automating a handoff, you notice that the receiving facility does not appear in the automated coordination system. What types of facilities commonly lack automated coordination, and what should you use instead?

replacements.question_text: When automating a handoff, you notice that the receiving facility does not appear in the automated coordination system. What types of facilities commonly lack automated coordination, and what should you use instead?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-6(a) identifies international facilities and VFR towers as examples of facilities where automated coordination may not be available. In these cases, manual coordination status must be annotated using the Coordination Menu or a flight progress strip, in accordance with facility directives.

choices.text: Any facility using a different automation platform, regardless of type. Use voice coordination only.
International facilities or VFR towers are examples. Use the Coordination Menu or a flight progress strip to annotate manual coordination, per facility directives.
Only military facilities. Use the Coordination Menu exclusively, as flight progress strips are not authorized.
Any facility outside the ARTCC's airspace boundary. Use an LOA-prescribed verbal relay procedure.

match.question_text: A controller enters important control information in the ACL Free Text Area about an aircraft that will be handed off in five minutes. Will the receiving sector automatically see this information after the handoff?

replacements.question_text: A controller enters important control information in the ACL Free Text Area about an aircraft that will be handed off in five minutes. Will the receiving sector automatically see this information after the handoff?
replacements.explanation: Per the note to 13-1-8, information entered into the Free Text Area does not pass on handoff. If the information needs to reach the receiving controller, it must be separately coordinated. The Free Text Area is for reference purposes only at the entering sector.

choices.text: No. Free Text Area information does not pass on handoff and must be separately coordinated if needed by the receiving sector.
Yes. All ACL data, including Free Text entries, transfers automatically with the aircraft on handoff.
Yes, but only if the Free Text Area remains open and visible at the time of handoff.
No, but the information will appear in the receiving sector's message queue for manual retrieval.

replacements.explanation: 13-1-8(e) states control information entered in the Free Text Area must be used for reference purposes only. It is not a substitute for official coordination, a permanent record, or handoff data.

choices.text: As an official coordination record that replaces verbal briefing on handoff.
As a primary clearance delivery method to the pilot via ACARS.
For reference purposes only.
As a permanent flight record that remains in the EAS archive.

replacements.explanation: 13-1-8(b) requires removing the yellow color coding on the ACL when a verbal point out has been approved. This is a specific ACL action distinct from general highlighting or Free Text entries.

choices.text: Enter the point out approval notation in the Free Text Area of the ACL.
Remove the yellow color coding on the ACL.
Highlight the entry on the ACL to indicate the point out has been approved.

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is an authorized source for abbreviations used in the ACL or DL Free Text Area?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-8(c) lists five authorized sources: TBL 13-1-1 clearance abbreviations, TBL 13-1-2 miscellaneous abbreviations, TBL 13-1-3 EDST equivalents, plain language markings, and locally approved abbreviations. Pilot shorthand is not authorized.

choices.text: Any abbreviation commonly used by pilots in their filed remarks.
Personal shorthand developed by the controller, as long as the next sector can guess the meaning.

match.question_text: A Route Action Notification (RAN) appears on your display indicating a route processing error for an aircraft your sector has track control for. What must you do about this RAN?

replacements.question_text: A Route Action Notification (RAN) appears on your display indicating a route processing error for an aircraft your sector has track control for. What must you do about this RAN?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-9(g) requires Route Action Notifications to be amended at the first control position that displays the RAN, unless verbally coordinated or as specified in appropriate facility directives. Additionally, RAN coding must not be removed unless the sector has track control or it has been otherwise coordinated.

choices.text: Ignore the RAN. Route processing errors are handled automatically by the ERAM system without controller input.
Amend the RAN at your position—the first control position displaying it—unless verbally coordinated or specified otherwise in facility directives.
Wait for the next sector to handle it. Route Action Notifications are resolved at the destination facility.
Delete the RAN coding immediately to clear the display, then investigate the route error afterward.

match.question_text: A sector that does not have track control for a flight notices ERT coding on the display. Another controller at that sector wants to send the ERT route acknowledgement to clear the coding. Is this authorized?

replacements.question_text: A sector that does not have track control for a flight notices ERT coding on the display. Another controller at that sector wants to send the ERT route acknowledgement to clear the coding. Is this authorized?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-9(f) states do not send or acknowledge ERT coding unless the sector has track control for the flight or it has been otherwise coordinated. ERT coding must be handled by the first sector displaying it or by a sector with track control. A sector without track control that attempts to send or acknowledge ERT coding violates this requirement.

choices.text: Yes. Any sector that can see ERT coding is authorized to send or acknowledge it to clear the display.
Yes, but only if the ERT coding has been present on the display for more than 10 minutes.
No, but only because ERT coding must first be escalated to the operations supervisor for resolution.
No. ERT coding must not be sent or acknowledged unless the sector has track control for the flight or it has been otherwise coordinated.

replacements.explanation: 13-1-9(d) specifically states EDCT coding is acknowledged on the DL. IAFDOF and UTM are on the ACL (b, c). ERT and RAN coding is not acknowledged on the DL.

choices.text: EDCT coding.
IAFDOF coding.
UTM coding.
ERT coding.

match.question_text: Why must the Inappropriate Altitude for Direction of Flight (IAFDOF) feature normally remain in automatic mode rather than manual mode?

replacements.question_text: Why must the Inappropriate Altitude for Direction of Flight (IAFDOF) feature normally remain in automatic mode rather than manual mode?
replacements.explanation: 13-1-9(a) requires IAFDOF to be used in automatic mode (IAFDOF Manual deselected) unless a facility directive authorizes otherwise. Automatic mode ensures the system continuously monitors all flights for altitude-for-direction errors without relying on manual controller intervention to flag each case.

choices.text: Manual mode consumes excessive system resources and may slow down the EDST display for other functions.
Automatic mode ensures continuous system monitoring of all flights for altitude-for-direction errors. Manual mode is authorized only when a facility directive specifically permits it.
Automatic mode is faster at resolving IAFDOF conflicts without controller evaluation of each case.
Manual mode was deprecated in the most recent ERAM software update and will be removed entirely.

ATS Interfacility Data Communications (AIDC).
Electronic Flight Data including Electronic Flight Strips.

replacements.question_text: ATOP provides controllers with automated decision support tools to establish, monitor and maintain separation between aircraft, and between aircraft and what?
replacements.explanation: 13-2-1(a) states ATOP provides decision support for separation between aircraft, and aircraft to airspace and terrain. This is broader than the typical EDST scope which only covers airspace.

choices.text: Other aircraft only, because ATOP separation logic does not extend to airspace or terrain.
Naval vessels in oceanic transit corridors.
Aircraft to airspace and terrain.

match.question_text: In ATOP oceanic airspace where conflict probe is being used as a decision support tool, what assumption must controllers make about the conflict probe separation calculations?

replacements.question_text: In ATOP oceanic airspace where conflict probe is being used as a decision support tool, what assumption must controllers make about the conflict probe separation calculations?
replacements.explanation: 13-2-2(a)(1)(a) requires controllers to assume that conflict probe separation calculations are accurate. This is the foundational assumption—controllers must utilize probe results to initiate and maintain prescribed separation minima unless one of the four override conditions in 13-2-2(a)(3) applies.

choices.text: That the calculations are advisory only and may be disregarded at the controller's discretion.
That the calculations are conservative and always overstate the actual conflict risk by a safety margin.
That the calculations are accurate, forming the basis for using probe to initiate and maintain prescribed separation minima.
That the calculations are valid only when the aircraft are both ADS-C equipped with contracts active.

match.question_text: Under 13-2-2 NOTE, once a trial probe is initiated, it does not take into account any changes made after that point. What must the controller still do when issuing a clearance or accepting a transfer?

replacements.question_text: Once a trial probe is initiated, it does not take into account any changes made after that point. What must the controller still do when issuing a clearance or accepting a transfer?
replacements.explanation: The trial probe note explicitly states: 'A trial probe does not alleviate the controller from performing a conflict probe when issuing a clearance or accepting a transfer.' Trial probe is a planning tool; conflict probe is the operational requirement.

choices.text: Rely on the trial probe results since they capture the situation at the time of the probe, and no additional probe is needed before issuing the clearance.
Wait until the trial probe expires and then issue the clearance without any further probe check.
Perform a conflict probe when issuing a clearance or accepting a transfer. The trial probe does not replace this requirement.
Use additional decision support tools instead of either trial probe or conflict probe when accepting transfers.

match.question_text: Under 13-2-2(a)(3), how many situations are listed in which controllers may override conflict probe?

replacements.question_text: (3), how many situations are listed in which controllers may override conflict probe?
replacements.explanation: 13-2-2(a)(3) lists four situations: (1) application of a separation standard not recognized by probe, (2) action taken to resolve the conflict and separation ensured, (3) control responsibility delegated to another sector/facility, and (4) other situations specified in facility directives.

choices.text: Four: unrecognized separation standard, conflict actions taken with separation ensured, control delegated, and situations specified in facility directives.
Two: only when probe does not recognize the separation standard or when facility directives say so.
Three: when separation standard is unrecognized, when control is delegated, or when facility directives say so.
Six: the four listed plus controller judgment override and pilot-requested override.

choices.text: Standard en route radar separation of 5 NM between aircraft on the same route at the same altitude.
ADS-C distance-based longitudinal separation at the standard reporting interval.
VFR Climb and Descent under paragraph 8-8-5.
Standard oceanic longitudinal separation using Mach Number Technique on the same track.

replacements.explanation: 13-2-2(b)(1) authorizes additional tools use for separation standards NOT supported by probe, or when probe is unavailable. It also allows use for situational awareness. But (b)(2) prohibits using additional tools to override probe when the standard IS supported.

choices.text: The separation standard is not supported by probe, or when probe is unavailable.
The controller personally prefers the appearance of the additional tools over the conflict probe display.
The conflict probe results disagree with the controller's expectation, even when the standard is supported.
The trial probe was run more than five minutes ago and may no longer be current.

replacements.question_text: The ASD title bar changes color to denote when priority information is being obscured or is out of view. What does this help the controller maintain awareness of?
replacements.explanation: The note explains the title bar color-change function is an alerting mechanism for when priority information on the ASD is being obscured or out of view. It helps the controller know when to reorganize windows to restore visibility.

That a red conflict has been detected in the controller's sector.

replacements.explanation: 13-2-4(c)(4) requires advising the pilot as early as possible to facilitate a smooth transition to voice. Workload permitting, the reason for termination should also be provided. This is a proactive duty.

Send an automatic CPDLC disconnect message and let the automation handle the transition without pilot notification.

replacements.question_text: The ATOP weather deviation tool adds a CDI mileage buffer to required separation calculations. What does CDI stand for?
replacements.explanation: CDI stands for Converging, Diverging, and Intersecting. The weather deviation tool adds this additional mileage buffer to the required separation when RNP distance-based longitudinal separation is being applied. The CDI buffer is enforced by ATOP conflict probe.

Conflict Detection Interface.
Converging, Diverging, and Intersecting.
Clearance Delivery Instruction.

match.question_text: At a multiple-controller ATOP workstation, the team has a pending red conflict resolution, three non-conformant messages in the error queue, and an administrative coordination request. What must govern how the team prioritizes and accomplishes these tasks?

replacements.question_text: At a multiple-controller ATOP workstation, the team has a pending red conflict resolution, three non-conformant messages in the error queue, and an administrative coordination request. What must govern how the team prioritizes and accomplishes these tasks?
replacements.explanation: 13-2-7 requires all ATC tasks to be completed according to their priority of duties, and the multiple-controller operation itself must be accomplished according to facility directives. Priority of duties governs task ordering—higher-priority duties like conflict resolution take precedence over administrative tasks. Facility directives govern how the team operates together.

choices.text: Tasks may be completed in any order as long as all are done by end of shift. Facility directives for team operations are guidelines only.

match.question_text: A controller is preparing to use PDC for departure clearances. Which set of governing documents controls how PDC must be utilized?

replacements.explanation: 14-1-1(a) establishes a three-source compliance chain for PDC: JO 7110.65 (this order), the local facility directive, and automated clearances developed under JO 7210.3 (Facility Operation and Administration). All three sources apply — none can be omitted.

choices.text: JO 7210.3 alone, because it governs all automated clearance development.
Only the local facility directive, which may override the order for PDC procedures.
This order (JO 7110.65), the local facility directive, and automated clearances developed in accordance with JO 7210.3.

choices.text: Compliance with the filed equipment suffix and the pilot's requested altitude.
Formatting correctness and ATIS broadcast verification.

replacements.explanation: 14-1-2(a) establishes the same three-source compliance chain for CPDLC DCL as 14-1-1(a) does for PDC: JO 7110.65 (this order), the local facility directive, and clearances developed in accordance with JO 7210.3.

Only the local facility directive, which establishes specific CPDLC procedures for each facility.

match.question_text: A departure has an amended flight plan with a revised route. The CPDLC DCL system is available and the aircraft is CPDLC-capable. What determines whether the amendment is transmitted by CPDLC or issued verbally?

replacements.question_text: A departure has an amended flight plan with a revised route. The CPDLC DCL system is available and the aircraft is CPDLC-capable. What determines whether the amendment is transmitted by CPDLC or issued verbally?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-2(c) is a key operational distinction from PDC: CPDLC permits amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted, unlike PDC which never permits them. However, if the amendment cannot be delivered using CPDLC (e.g., because the required data isn't in the TAS drop-down menus), it must be verbally issued. The determining factor is whether CPDLC can actually deliver the amendment.

choices.text: Whether the amended plan contains a SID; if so, it must always be verbally issued regardless of CPDLC capability.
Whether the amendment was generated by automation in the tower; automation-generated changes are always CPDLC-eligible.
Whether the ATIS is currently broadcasting CPDLC availability; verbal issuance is required only during the 60-day commissioning window.
Whether the amendment can be delivered using CPDLC; if it cannot, the clearance must be verbally issued.

match.question_text: When preparing a CPDLC DCL for transmission, what must the controller do beyond reviewing the clearance for accuracy and route integrity?

replacements.question_text: When preparing a CPDLC DCL for transmission, what must the controller do beyond reviewing the clearance for accuracy and route integrity?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-2(b) mirrors PDC's review requirements: the controller must review for accuracy and route integrity, and must also take action to ensure all information is complete, understandable to the recipient, and the route of flight is continuous. 'Action must be taken' makes this an affirmative duty, not a passive check.

choices.text: Ensure all information is complete and understandable to the recipient and the route of flight is continuous.
Confirm the clearance has been acknowledged by the receiving CPDLC ground station before transmission.
Verify the ATIS message includes the CPDLC availability broadcast before sending any DCL.
Log each DCL transmission in the facility shift log for later audit.

match.question_text: A controller is entering data in the DCL application. Under what circumstance is the information in Selectable Field 6 (the Expected Altitude Field) intended to be used?

replacements.question_text: A controller is entering data in the DCL application. Under what circumstance is the information in Selectable Field 6 (the Expected Altitude Field) intended to be used?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(6): Field 6 is the Expected Altitude Field, reserved for specifying when the Expected Altitude would be used — specifically in the event of lost communications. This is a contingency field that provides the pilot with altitude guidance in case two-way radio communication is lost after departure.

choices.text: When the aircraft is cleared to climb via the SID and needs a top altitude specified.
When the departure frequency changes after takeoff and the new controller needs the expected altitude.
For the ATIS broadcast to include the expected altitude in the departure information.
In the event of lost communications, to specify when the Expected Altitude would be used.

replacements.question_text: A controller is populating Selectable Field 7 (the Departure Frequency Field) for a departure on a SID that includes a published departure control frequency. When may the selection 'SEE SID' be used instead of entering a specific frequency?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(7): SEE SID may be used as an alternative to entering a specific frequency when the SID contains a Departure Control Frequency Assignment specific to the intended departure procedure. It directs the pilot to reference the SID chart for the frequency.

choices.text: When the controller does not know the departure frequency.
For all departures at facilities without a dedicated departure control position.
If the SID contains Departure Control Frequency Assignment specific to the intended departure procedure.
When the clearance is transmitted by CPDLC rather than PDC.

match.question_text: A controller needs to include additional contact information in a DCL. Which field is reserved for this purpose, and what governs its content and character limit?

replacements.question_text: A controller needs to include additional contact information in a DCL. Which field is reserved for this purpose, and what governs its content and character limit?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(8): Field 8 (the Contact Field) is reserved for additional contact information. Its content is governed by facility directives, and it is limited to 32 characters. This is distinct from Field 9 which has a 34-character limit and is for local information.

choices.text: Content governed by facility directives; limited to 32 characters.
Content governed by JO 7210.3; limited to 34 characters.
Content governed by the TAS predefined menu only; limited to 28 characters.

match.question_text: A controller wants to enter a heading assignment in Selectable Field 3 (the Climb Out Field) for a departure on the BMRNG2 SID, which publishes a climb via heading 270. What constraint governs what can be entered in this field?

replacements.question_text: A controller wants to enter a heading assignment in Selectable Field 3 (the Climb Out Field) for a departure on the BMRNG2 SID, which publishes a climb via heading 270. What constraint governs what can be entered in this field?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(3) imposes three constraints on the Climb Out Field: (1) instructions must never contradict SID instructions, (2) the field may reiterate pertinent SID information, and (3) it is limited to 32 characters with only TAS-adapted entries available.

choices.text: Never contradict SID instructions; limited to 32 characters.

replacements.question_text: A departure flight plan includes a correctly filed SID. When populating the DCL application, what must Selectable Field 1 (the SID Field) contain?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(1)(a) specifies that Field 1 must contain the correctly filed SID when one is filed. The other two options for Field 1 are: (b) the SID assigned by EAS, or (c) NO SID if none is filed or assigned and none is to be assigned.

choices.text: The SID assigned by the EAS, regardless of what was filed.
The correctly filed SID.
Any SID that provides vertical guidance for the departure.
NO SID, with the actual SID entered in the Climb Out Field instead.

match.question_text: A departure is assigned a SID that contains vertical guidance from take-off all the way to a published altitude to maintain. The aircraft is expected to follow the SID as published. What must Selectable Field 4 contain?

replacements.question_text: A departure is assigned a SID that contains vertical guidance from take-off all the way to a published altitude to maintain. The aircraft is expected to follow the SID as published. What must Selectable Field 4 contain?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(4)(a): when the SID has vertical guidance from take-off to an altitude to maintain, and the aircraft should follow the SID, Field 4 must contain CLIMB VIA SID. This is the simplest of three Field 4 conditions — no EXCEPT clause is needed because the SID altitude matches the clearance.

choices.text: MAINTAIN (assigned altitude), placed in Field 5 instead.
CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude), because any SID with vertical guidance always requires the EXCEPT clause.
Leave Field 4 blank since Field 1 already identifies the SID.

replacements.question_text: A departure is assigned a SID that provides vertical guidance but does not include an initial altitude to maintain. What must Selectable Field 4 contain in this situation?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(4)(b): when the SID has vertical guidance but no initial altitude to maintain, Field 4 must contain CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude). This distinguishes it from condition (a) where the SID has both vertical guidance and an altitude to maintain (use CLIMB VIA SID), and condition (c) where the assigned altitude differs from the published altitude.

choices.text: CLIMB VIA SID, because the SID has vertical guidance regardless of altitude.
CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude).
MAINTAIN (assigned altitude) in Field 4, since no SID altitude exists.
Leave Field 4 blank and place the altitude in Field 3.

replacements.question_text: A departure is assigned a SID with a published altitude to maintain, but the controller needs to assign a different altitude. How should the controller handle this in the DCL application?
replacements.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(4)(c) provides an amendment mechanism: if the assigned altitude differs from the published altitude in the SID, the altitude may be amended using CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude). This allows the controller to modify the SID altitude without overriding the SID selection itself.

choices.text: Overwrite the SID altitude in Field 1 with the new value.
Amend the altitude using CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude).
Issue a verbal amendment to the flight crew and disregard Field 4.

match.question_text: A controller wants to use CPDLC to send an altimeter setting and a frequency change to an aircraft. Are these types of communications approved for CPDLC augmentation of voice?

replacements.question_text: A controller wants to use CPDLC to send an altimeter setting and a frequency change to an aircraft. Are these types of communications approved for CPDLC augmentation of voice?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-1(a) lists seven categories that CPDLC is approved to augment: altitudes, routes, speeds, holding clearances, altimeters, advisories, and frequency changes. Both altimeter settings and frequency changes are explicitly included. CPDLC augments — but does not replace — voice communication for these items.

choices.text: Both altimeter settings and frequency changes are among the categories CPDLC is approved to augment.
Only altimeter settings are approved for CPDLC; frequency changes must be by voice.
Neither is approved; CPDLC is limited to route and altitude clearances only.
Frequency changes are approved; altimeter settings must be issued by voice only.

match.question_text: A supervisor at a position other than the sector with CPDLC eligibility needs to terminate a CPDLC connection. What must happen before the termination can proceed?

replacements.question_text: A supervisor at a position other than the sector with CPDLC eligibility needs to terminate a CPDLC connection. What must happen before the termination can proceed?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-1(h): coordination must be accomplished with the sector that has eligibility before terminating a CPDLC connection from any other position or adapted air traffic workstation. This coordination requirement ensures the sector working the aircraft is aware of and agrees to the termination.

choices.text: Coordination must be accomplished with the sector with eligibility prior to termination.
No coordination is needed if the termination is from a supervisor position.
Coordination is only needed with the receiving sector, not the sector with eligibility.

match.question_text: An aircraft is approaching the boundary of the continental United States and will be transferred to an ATOP oceanic sector. What must the last controller do regarding the CPDLC connection, unless otherwise coordinated?

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is approaching the boundary of the continental United States and will be transferred to an ATOP oceanic sector. What must the last controller do regarding the CPDLC connection, unless otherwise coordinated?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-1(g): unless otherwise coordinated, the last controller working the aircraft before it exits the continental U.S. must ensure the CPDLC connection is terminated upon transfer to any non-U.S. facility or ATOP sector. The connection does not automatically transfer to non-U.S. or ATOP systems.

choices.text: Transfer the CPDLC connection seamlessly to the non-U.S. facility.
Pre-establish a new CPDLC connection with the receiving oceanic sector.
Terminate the CPDLC connection upon transfer of communication to the non-U.S. facility or ATOP sector.
Instruct the pilot to terminate the CPDLC connection upon entering oceanic airspace.

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is on final approach in IMC conditions, and the controller has a routine altitude amendment to send. Should the controller use CPDLC to transmit this clearance?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-1(c) states controllers should minimize the use of CPDLC during critical phases of flight. Final approach is a critical phase where the pilot's attention should be on flying the aircraft, not reading data link messages. The controller should use voice for time-sensitive clearances during critical phases.

choices.text: The controller should minimize CPDLC use during critical phases; using voice may be more appropriate.
Yes, CPDLC augments voice for all clearances including during approach.
No, because CPDLC should not be used to issue immediate clearances, but routine amendments are always permitted by CPDLC.
Yes, because CPDLC is mandatory for all altitude assignments regardless of flight phase.

replacements.question_text: During an en route CPDLC session, a pilot sends a route request downlink. Who bears the responsibility for sending and responding to this and other CPDLC messages for that aircraft?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-1(b) assigns responsibility for sending and responding to CPDLC messages to the sector team. This is an explicit assignment — CPDLC message handling is not delegated to a separate data link position unless specifically adapted.

choices.text: The CPDLC system administrator, who monitors data link traffic sector-wide.
The facility's automation specialist, who handles all CPDLC message routing.
The flight data position, which processes all non-voice communications.

match.question_text: A controller needs to override an open CPDLC altitude clearance by voice and issue a new altitude. What is the prescribed phraseology for overriding a CPDLC clearance?

replacements.question_text: A controller needs to override an open CPDLC altitude clearance by voice and issue a new altitude. What is the prescribed phraseology for overriding a CPDLC clearance?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-2(c) provides the exact phraseology: DISREGARD CPDLC (type) CLEARANCE (description of clearance) AND SEND AN UNABLE (alternate clearance). This phraseology is prescribed because it tells the pilot which specific clearance to disregard and requires the UNABLE response to close the uplink on the flight deck.

CANCEL CPDLC (type) CLEARANCE AND ACKNOWLEDGE (alternate clearance).
OVERRIDE CPDLC (type) CLEARANCE, NEW CLEARANCE (alternate clearance).
DISREGARD CPDLC MESSAGE AND COMPLY WITH (alternate clearance).

match.question_text: An abnormal CPDLC indication appears in the FDB for an aircraft. A trainee reaches to acknowledge it immediately. What must happen before the controller acknowledges this indication?

replacements.question_text: An abnormal CPDLC indication appears in the FDB for an aircraft. A trainee reaches to acknowledge it immediately. What must happen before the controller acknowledges this indication?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-2(b): abnormal CPDLC indications must be acknowledged only after required coordination has been performed. The sequence is important — coordination first, then acknowledgment. Acknowledging before coordination could result in operational errors because other sectors may not yet be aware of the situation.

choices.text: The indication should be acknowledged immediately to clear the FDB for operational continuity.
Required coordination must be performed before the indication is acknowledged.
The pilot must acknowledge the abnormal indication via voice before the controller can acknowledge it.

replacements.question_text: An aircraft has lost two-way voice communication capability (NORDO) but still has an active CPDLC connection. What is the sector with CPDLC eligibility permitted to do?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-2(e)(1): for NORDO aircraft with an active CPDLC connection, the sector with eligibility may mark the aircraft on frequency to allow CPDLC communications. This is an exception to normal procedure — CPDLC can serve as a backup communication path for aircraft that have lost voice capability but retain data link.

choices.text: Mark the aircraft on frequency to allow CPDLC communications with that aircraft.
Terminate the CPDLC connection and rely on light gun signals for all further communication.

match.question_text: When a controller overrides a CPDLC clearance by voice, why must the pilot be instructed to send an UNABLE response?

replacements.question_text: When a controller overrides a CPDLC clearance by voice, why must the pilot be instructed to send an UNABLE response?
replacements.explanation: The notes to 14-2-2(d) explain: canceling an uplink from the ground only removes it from the CPDLC ground system — the uplink remains open on the flight deck. The pilot must send UNABLE to close the uplink on the flight deck. Additionally, the CPDLC clearance may not have been received on the flight deck at the time of the voice override.

choices.text: UNABLE is required only for FANS 1/A aircraft that cannot process ROGER responses.
Canceling an uplink only removes it from the ground system; the pilot must send UNABLE to close it on the flight deck.
The UNABLE response is a formality; the system closes the uplink automatically when the controller cancels it.
It is optional; the pilot may send WILCO or ROGER instead, whichever is more appropriate.

match.question_text: A controller notices that an aircraft has not received an automated altimeter setting. Under what conditions does the CPDLC system automatically send altimeter settings to aircraft?

replacements.question_text: A controller notices that an aircraft has not received an automated altimeter setting. Under what conditions does the CPDLC system automatically send altimeter settings to aircraft?
replacements.explanation: The note to 14-2-3(a) states automated altimeters are only sent in response to two specific events: a monitor transfer of communication (TOC), or an altitude uplink when the assigned altitude is below FL 180. choices.text: A monitor transfer of communication (TOC) and an initial contact message from the aircraft.
A monitor transfer of communication (TOC), or an altitude uplink when the assigned altitude is below FL 180.
An altitude uplink when above FL 180 and a pilot-initiated downlink requesting the setting.
Every frequency change and every transfer of communication, regardless of altitude.

match.question_text: An aircraft's CPDLC connection is unexpectedly lost mid-sector and voice communication had not been established with that aircraft prior to the loss. What must the controller do?

replacements.question_text: An aircraft's CPDLC connection is unexpectedly lost mid-sector and voice communication had not been established with that aircraft prior to the loss. What must the controller do?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-3(b): when CPDLC connection is unexpectedly lost and voice had not been previously established, the controller must ensure voice communication is established and maintained. choices.text: Ensure voice communication is established and maintained with that aircraft.
Wait for the aircraft to re-establish the CPDLC connection automatically.
Send a CPDLC free-text message requesting the aircraft to re-establish connection.

match.question_text: The CPDLC system is being shut down and there are several aircraft with open uplinks. How must controllers resolve these open or abnormally closed uplinks?

replacements.question_text: The CPDLC system is being shut down and there are several aircraft with open uplinks. How must controllers resolve these open or abnormally closed uplinks?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-3(c)(2): controllers must take action to resolve any open or abnormally closed uplinks, by voice, with each aircraft at the time of shutdown. match.question_text: A controller is composing a weather-related advisory message via CPDLC free text and wants to abbreviate 'moderate turbulence.' Which FAA Order governs the use of abbreviations in CPDLC messages?

replacements.question_text: A controller is composing a weather-related advisory message via CPDLC free text and wants to abbreviate 'moderate turbulence.' Which FAA Order governs the use of abbreviations in CPDLC messages?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-4(a)(2): when using abbreviations to compose weather-related or advisory/free text messages, controllers must comply with FAA Order JO 7340.2, Contractions. This is a specific cross-reference. Examples from the note include MOD for moderate and TURB for turbulence.

match.question_text: An aircraft is established on the FYRBD2 arrival, which has published speed restrictions at several waypoints. A controller wants to issue a speed adjustment using CPDLC. Is this permitted?

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is established on the FYRBD2 arrival, which has published speed restrictions at several waypoints. A controller wants to issue a speed adjustment using CPDLC. Is this permitted?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-4(b)(2): CPDLC must not be used to issue a speed adjustment to an aircraft established on a route or procedure that has published speed restrictions. The prohibition is absolute ('must not'). The controller must use voice to issue speed adjustments in this situation.

Yes, but only if the aircraft is above FL 390 where WILCO satisfies pilot concurrence.

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is holding at a published fix and needs to be cleared out of holding to continue to its destination. Why must this clearance be issued via voice rather than CPDLC?
replacements.explanation: The note to 14-2-4(c)(1): a route uplink does not specify a new clearance limit. Since clearing an aircraft out of holding requires providing a new clearance limit, and CPDLC route uplinks cannot convey this, the clearance must be done via voice.

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is on a descend via clearance and needs holding instructions at a fix along the STAR. The controller wants to issue the holding instructions via CPDLC. What must be done first?
replacements.explanation: 14-2-4(c)(2) and its note: if an aircraft has a climb/descend via clearance, holding instructions must not be issued via CPDLC. replacements.question_text: A CPDLC-connected aircraft is operating outside VHF coverage with an active data link connection. The controller determines that issuing a complex reroute by voice would be faster and clearer than building it in CPDLC. Is voice communication permitted?
replacements.explanation: 14-3-1(b): voice communications may be utilized for CPDLC aircraft when it will provide an operational advantage and/or when workload or equipment capabilities demand. choices.text: No, voice is never permitted for CPDLC-connected aircraft outside VHF coverage.

match.question_text: Why does using the standard CPDLC message set, rather than free text, ensure the proper closure of CPDLC exchanges?

replacements.question_text: Why does using the standard CPDLC message set, rather than free text, ensure the proper closure of CPDLC exchanges?
replacements.explanation: The note to 14-3-1: the CPDLC message set is designed with defined response attributes (W/U, Y, N, etc.) that structure each exchange. When a message from the set is sent, the system knows what response is expected and can track whether the exchange is complete. Free text messages lack this structured closure mechanism.

choices.text: Free text messages require ICAO document 10037 approval, which is not available for oceanic operations.
The message set ensures proper closure of CPDLC exchanges through defined response attributes.
The message set is faster to transmit over SATCOM voice backup channels.
Free text messages cannot be received by FANS 1/A aircraft outside VHF coverage.
