match.question_text: A specific aircraft loses its CPDLC connection while operating in oceanic airspace. The connection was previously active and the aircraft is still on frequency. What options does the controller have to restore CPDLC?

replacements.question_text: A specific aircraft loses its CPDLC connection while operating in oceanic airspace. The connection was previously active and the aircraft is still on frequency. What options does the controller have to restore CPDLC?
replacements.explanation: 14-3-3(c): the controller has two options when CPDLC connection is lost on a specific aircraft: send a connection request message (CR1) to attempt automatic reconnection, or advise the pilot via backup communications (HF, SATCOM, Third Party) to log on again. Both are 'should' actions.

choices.text: Wait for the aircraft to automatically reconnect; no controller action is required.
Terminate CPDLC service for all aircraft in the sector until the specific aircraft reconnects.
Transfer the aircraft to the next facility immediately, since no CPDLC connection means the aircraft cannot be worked.
Send a connection request message (CR1) or advise the pilot via backup communications to log on again.

match.question_text: After a period of CPDLC delivery uncertainty, a controller re-establishes voice contact with an aircraft to verify whether a previously sent clearance was received. What phraseology should preface the controller's message on initial voice contact?

replacements.question_text: After a period of CPDLC delivery uncertainty, a controller re-establishes voice contact with an aircraft to verify whether a previously sent clearance was received. What phraseology should preface the controller's message on initial voice contact?
replacements.explanation: 14-3-3(e) prescribes the phraseology: '(Call Sign) CPDLC Failure, (message).' This specific phraseology is used when there was uncertainty that a CPDLC clearance was delivered, to immediately alert the pilot that the communication relates to a potential CPDLC delivery issue.

choices.text: "CPDLC Delivery Uncertain, verify receipt of last clearance."
"CPDLC Failure, (message)."
"Disregard CPDLC clearance, verify last transmission."
"CPDLC System Error, confirm clearance received."

match.question_text: An automated CPDLC transfer to the next facility has failed. After the controller attempts a manual transfer and is unable to complete it, what should the controller do next?

replacements.question_text: An automated CPDLC transfer to the next facility has failed. After the controller attempts a manual transfer and is unable to complete it, what should the controller do next?
replacements.explanation: 14-3-3(a) establishes a two-step escalation: first, attempt to initiate the transfer manually. If unable to complete the manual transfer, then advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an End Service (EOS) message. The EOS closes out the current connection cleanly.

choices.text: Terminate the CPDLC connection and revert all communications to voice for the remainder of the flight.
Send a connection request (CR1) and wait for the system to reconcile the transfer automatically.
Advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an End Service (EOS) message.
Transfer the aircraft using only voice procedures and cancel the CPDLC service entirely.

match.question_text: CPDLC service is going to be canceled for an oceanic aircraft. What are the controller's obligations regarding advising the pilot?

replacements.question_text: CPDLC service is going to be canceled for an oceanic aircraft. What are the controller's obligations regarding advising the pilot?
replacements.explanation: 14-3-3(d) contains a must/should distinction: the controller MUST advise the pilot as early as possible to facilitate a smooth transition to voice (mandatory). Workload permitting, the controller SHOULD also advise the reason for termination (discretionary).

choices.text: May cancel CPDLC without advising the pilot, as the aircraft will detect the termination automatically.
Must advise the pilot as early as possible; should advise the reason workload permitting.
Must provide the reason for termination in all cases, regardless of workload.
Should wait until the aircraft is within VHF coverage before advising of the cancellation.

match.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the ATC system?

replacements.question_text: What is the primary purpose of the ATC system?
replacements.explanation: The primary purpose of the ATC system is preventing collisions involving aircraft operating in the system. Additional services and expeditious flow matter, but they do not replace that primary purpose.

choices.text: Provide the most expeditious movement for every aircraft regardless of separation needs.
Prevent collisions involving aircraft operating in the system.
Provide additional services whenever pilots request them, regardless of workload.
Support National Security and Homeland Defense as the system's only purpose.

replacements.explanation: The paragraph allows exceeding the surface-area speed limit subject to the 250-knot cap unless the pilot informs the controller that a higher minimum speed is required. Since the pilot has informed the controller of the requirement, the 250-knot cap exception applies.

Approve only 250 knots because the note does not mention flight manual recommendations as a valid exception.
Approve 275 knots because the pilot has stated the higher speed is required by the flight manual, which satisfies the exception.

match.question_text: A pilot calls the tower on initial contact and the controller responds with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby.' The controller is then distracted by an emergency and cannot provide services for several minutes. The pilot, having heard 'standby,' enters the Class D. Is the pilot's entry permitted?

replacements.question_text: A pilot calls the tower on initial contact and the controller responds with the aircraft call sign followed by 'standby.' The controller is then distracted by an emergency and cannot provide services for several minutes. The pilot, having heard 'standby,' enters the Class D. Is the pilot's entry permitted?
replacements.explanation: The paragraph says that when the controller responds with the call sign and 'standby,' communications are established and the pilot CAN enter. However, if workload prevents immediate service provision, the controller must tell the pilot to REMAIN OUTSIDE. In this scenario, the controller said only 'standby' — which does establish communications and legally permits entry — but the controller should have used the 'remain outside' instruction to keep the pilot out. Since the controller only said 'standby,' the pilot's entry was technically permitted.

choices.text: No, because any gap in controller attention automatically revokes the prior communication establishment.
Yes, because 'standby' with the call sign established two-way communications, which is all that is required for Class D entry.
No, because the controller intended to delay services, which implicitly means the pilot must remain outside.
Yes, but only if the pilot first requests and receives an explicit 'enter the Delta' clearance.

In both cases communications are established, but the second adds an instruction that keeps the aircraft outside until services can be provided.
'Standby' alone does not establish communications; only the longer phraseology establishes communications.
The first response denies entry; the second permits entry after a delay.

The controller may use 'PROCEED AS REQUESTED' with additional instructions as needed.

choices.text: Authorize the operation only after coordinating with approach control for each arrival.
Authorize the operation using 'PROCEED AS REQUESTED' with any necessary additional instructions.
Deny the request because RSA operations are never permitted while aircraft are arriving.
Tell the vehicle to wait until all arriving aircraft have landed and cleared the runway.

replacements.explanation: Subparagraph a covers non-aircraft (vehicles, equipment, personnel) on or near the movement area that pilots need to recognize. Subparagraph b covers the relative position of other aircraft (traffic) in relation to the receiving aircraft, using easy-to-understand terms like 'to your right' or 'ahead of you.'

choices.text: Subparagraph a requires radar; subparagraph b requires visual observation.
Subparagraph a is for non-aircraft hazards; subparagraph b is for describing other aircraft positions relative to the receiving aircraft.
Subparagraph a is for ground operations; subparagraph b is for airborne operations only.
There is no distinction — both cover the same types of information.

match.question_text: A trainee controller observes an aircraft on the ground receive a steady green signal and tells the pilot it means 'cleared to taxi.' A more experienced controller corrects this. What does steady green actually mean to an aircraft on the ground under TBL 3-2-1?

replacements.question_text: A trainee controller observes an aircraft on the ground receive a steady green signal and tells the pilot it means 'cleared to taxi.' A more experienced controller corrects this. What does steady green actually mean to an aircraft on the ground?
replacements.explanation: Steady green to an aircraft on the ground means 'Cleared for takeoff.' Flashing green means 'Cleared to taxi.' Confusing these two is a common and dangerous error because it could result in an aircraft taking the runway when only cleared to taxi.

choices.text: Cleared to taxi — the trainee was correct.
Cleared for takeoff.
Return for landing, to be followed by steady green at the proper time.

replacements.explanation: For vehicles, equipment, and personnel, steady green means 'Cleared to cross; proceed; go.' It is not 'cleared to taxi' which is the aircraft-on-ground meaning of flashing green. This is a common confusion because the signal-column meanings differ by recipient type.

choices.text: Cleared to cross; proceed; go.
Cleared to taxi, same as for an aircraft on the ground.

replacements.explanation: Flashing red means 'Taxi clear of landing area or runway in use' for aircraft on the ground, but means 'Airport unsafe — Do not land' for aircraft in flight. These are completely different meanings that depend on whether the aircraft is airborne.

match.question_text: A controller issues an alternating red-and-green warning signal to an aircraft for a soft-field condition. The pilot, seeing red in the signal, comes to a complete stop and refuses to move. The pilot later argues that any signal containing red means 'stop.' Is the pilot's interpretation correct?

replacements.question_text: A controller issues an alternating red-and-green warning signal to an aircraft for a soft-field condition. The pilot, seeing red in the signal, comes to a complete stop and refuses to move. The pilot later argues that any signal containing red means 'stop.' Is the pilot's interpretation correct?
replacements.explanation: The note explicitly states the warning signal is 'not a prohibitive signal.' It is a caution that means 'exercise extreme caution,' not 'stop.' It can even be followed by another light signal. The pilot should exercise heightened alertness, not stop.

choices.text: Yes — any red component in an ATC light signal always requires the aircraft to stop.
Yes — the warning signal is prohibitive and means the aircraft must not move until a different signal is given.
No — the warning signal is not prohibitive; it means exercise extreme caution, and the aircraft may continue with heightened alertness.
No — but the aircraft should return to the starting point before continuing.

For a helicopter in flight during daylight, the correct acknowledgment is to 'either flash the landing light or rock the tip path plane.' The first controller's instruction was wrong; the second controller is correct that helicopters use different methods.

replacements.question_text: What is the key operational difference between the daylight acknowledgment for a hovering helicopter versus a helicopter in flight?
replacements.explanation: A hovering helicopter may either 'turn the helicopter toward the controlling facility and flash the landing light or rock the tip path plane.' A helicopter in flight may 'either flash the landing light or rock the tip path plane.' The hovering procedure adds the option of turning toward the facility as part of the acknowledgment.

choices.text: Hovering helicopters have the additional option of turning toward the controlling facility while flashing the landing light; in-flight helicopters do not have the turn option.
There is no difference — hovering and in-flight helicopters use identical acknowledgments.
Hovering helicopters must rock the wings; in-flight helicopters must flash the landing light.
In-flight helicopters may move ailerons or rudder; hovering helicopters cannot.

match.question_text: A controller receives an updated RwyCC for Runway 18: codes 4, 3, 3. The ATIS is still broadcasting the previous report. The controller decides to wait until the next ATIS update cycle rather than verbally issuing the new codes. Is this correct?

replacements.question_text: A controller receives an updated RwyCC for Runway 18: codes 4, 3, 3. The ATIS is still broadcasting the previous report. The controller decides to wait until the next ATIS update cycle rather than verbally issuing the new codes. Is this correct?
replacements.explanation: Paragraph 3-3-1e(1)(b) requires that when an update to the RwyCC is provided, the controller must verbally issue it to all aircraft until the ATIS broadcast can be updated. Waiting for the ATIS cycle without verbal issuance violates this requirement.

choices.text: Yes — ATIS is the designated distribution system and verbal issuance is optional.
Yes, but only if the change from the previous report involves a single code change.
No — the updated RwyCC must be verbally issued to aircraft until the ATIS can be updated.
No — but the controller need only issue it to arriving aircraft, not departures.

replacements.question_text: A pilot reports 'braking seems fair to ok' on Runway 9. No RwyCC is available. What should the controller issue to other aircraft?
replacements.explanation: In the absence of RwyCC, only factual information as reported by the airport operator or pilots concerning runway surface condition and describing accumulation of precipitation should be issued. The controller should relay the pilot's factual observation, converting nonstandard braking terminology only if 3-3-4 procedures apply. The controller does NOT convert to RwyCC.

Convert the pilot's words into an estimated RwyCC and issue that.
Hold the report until airport management confirms the condition and issues an official RwyCC.

replacements.question_text: Who is legally authorized to close a runway, and what action can a controller take if unable to reach that authority?
replacements.explanation: Only airport management or the military operations office can close a runway. The controller cannot close it but can issue a NOTAM publicizing an unsafe condition when management is unreachable. A NOTAM does not close the runway — it publicizes the condition.

The controller can close the runway for up to 30 minutes, after which airport management must take over.
Any FAA-certified employee at the facility can close a runway during emergencies.

No — the controller never issues a clearance; the controller only states the operation will be at the pilot's own risk.

No — the controller must deny the request entirely at this stage.

replacements.explanation: HIRL intensity settings are determined by time of day (day/night/twilight) and reported visibility or RVR. replacements.explanation: distinguishes between tower-controlled airports in Class B/C/TRSA/basic-radar-service areas (where landing triggers automatic termination) and all other airports (where being instructed to change to tower or advisory frequency triggers automatic termination). For non-tower airports, the instruction to change to advisory frequency is the trigger.

choices.text: Radar service continues until the aircraft reports on the ground.
The controller must issue 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' before the frequency change.
Radar service continues until the pilot cancels flight following.
Radar service is automatically terminated when instructed to change to advisory frequency.

match.question_text: What is the difference between the controller response when an assigned beacon code is not displayed versus when the transponder appears inoperative?

replacements.question_text: What is the difference between the controller response when an assigned beacon code is not displayed versus when the transponder appears inoperative?
replacements.explanation: For a non-displaying code with an operable transponder, the controller says 'RESET TRANSPONDER, SQUAWK (code).' For an apparently inoperative transponder, the controller says 'YOUR TRANSPONDER APPEARS INOPERATIVE/MALFUNCTIONING, RESET, SQUAWK (code).' Both require notification of the subsequent control position.

choices.text: Only the inoperative case requires notifying the subsequent control position.
The non-display case uses 'RESET TRANSPONDER' without the inoperative language; the inoperative case adds 'YOUR TRANSPONDER APPEARS INOPERATIVE/MALFUNCTIONING' before the reset instruction.
The non-displaying code case requires terminating radar service immediately.
There is no operational difference; the phraseology is identical for both situations.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-1-3a-d, the four items are: (a) wind, runway, and altimeter setting; (b) traffic information on a workload-permitting basis; (c) time or place to contact tower; and (d) the aircraft may be instructed to contact approach upon initial contact with tower. The item 'sequencing instructions' is not among the listed items.

choices.text: ATIS code, vector to final, approach clearance, and landing clearance.
Wind, runway, altimeter, and a mandatory traffic advisory for all known aircraft.
Wind/runway/altimeter, traffic information, tower contact instructions, and the option to direct initial tower contacts to approach.
Approach clearance, squawk code, frequency change, and wake turbulence caution.

choices.text: Only after the aircraft has entered the traffic pattern.
Upon initial contact with the tower.
Only after approach control has already issued the ATIS to the aircraft.

replacements.question_text: A nonapproach control tower accepts visual separation authorization for two IFR arrivals. Who retains responsibility for IFR separation before and after the visual separation is applied?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-2-1, while the nonapproach tower becomes responsible for the visual separation itself, separation of IFR aircraft before and after the application of visual separation is an IFR control function. This responsibility remains with the Approach/Departure/En Route facility.

choices.text: The nonapproach control tower, because accepting visual separation transfers all separation responsibility.
The pilot, because visual separation shifts responsibility to the flight crew.

replacements.question_text: An en route controller and a terminal controller each apply pilot-applied visual separation for aircraft on converging courses. How does the en route traffic advisory differ from the terminal version?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-2-1, the en route converging-course advisory includes the explicit words 'ON CONVERGING COURSE' in the transmission — e.g., '(ACID) TRAFFIC, (position), (direction)-BOUND, (type), ON CONVERGING COURSE, HAS YOU IN SIGHT AND WILL MAINTAIN VISUAL SEPARATION.' The terminal version does not include the 'ON CONVERGING COURSE' element.

choices.text: The en route version omits aircraft type; the terminal version includes it.
The en route version requires a vector; the terminal version does not.
The en route version includes 'ON CONVERGING COURSE' in the transmission; the terminal version does not.
There is no difference; both use identical phraseology.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-2-1a1(d), visual separation must not be applied between successive departures when departure routes and/or aircraft performance preclude maintaining separation. Even if the tower can see both aircraft, if their flight paths or performance differences would prevent maintaining visual separation, it cannot be used.

choices.text: When the departures are from different airports.
When the first departure is a heavy and the second is a small.

match.question_text: An aircraft is cleared to maintain VFR-on-top at the pilot's request. Which of the following correctly describes the pilot's obligations compared to a VFR-conditions clearance?

replacements.question_text: An aircraft is cleared to maintain VFR-on-top at the pilot's request. Which of the following correctly describes the pilot's obligations compared to a VFR-conditions clearance?
replacements.explanation: VFR-on-top (7-3-1) keeps the aircraft on its IFR flight plan. The pilot must: select an appropriate VFR cruising altitude per 91.159, comply with VFR visibility and cloud clearance criteria, see and avoid other aircraft, and comply with applicable IFR rules (e.g., adhere to ATC clearances). IFR separation is suspended but traffic advisories and safety alerts continue. VFR conditions (7-1-2) is a time/altitude-limited clearance with ATC-specified altitudes — it does not transfer altitude selection to the pilot.

choices.text: VFR-on-top keeps the aircraft on an IFR flight plan; the pilot selects altitude per 91.159 and must comply with both VFR visibility/cloud criteria and applicable IFR rules.
Both are identical; 'VFR-on-top' and 'VFR conditions' are interchangeable ATC terms.
VFR conditions cancels the IFR flight plan; VFR-on-top keeps it IFR but waives all IFR rule compliance.
VFR-on-top can only be used in Class A airspace; VFR conditions can only be used below FL180.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-3-1a, the pilot must be on an IFR flight plan. VFR-on-top is an IFR clearance that suspends IFR separation but does not cancel the IFR flight plan. The pilot must request it; the controller cannot initiate it.

Between sunset and sunrise, unless IFR vertical separation restrictions are applied.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-3-1d, the controller must issue an alternative clearance ensuring separation from all other aircraft for which the controller has separation responsibility. The phraseology is 'IF UNABLE, (alternative procedure), AND ADVISE.' This mirrors the obligation in 7-1-2b.

Wait for the pilot to report inability to continue VFR before taking any action.

match.question_text: A radar report indicates a VFR-on-top aircraft at 5,500 feet on a magnetic course of 270 degrees above 3,000 feet AGL. What action should the controller take?

replacements.question_text: A radar report indicates a VFR-on-top aircraft at 5,500 feet on a magnetic course of 270 degrees above 3,000 feet AGL. What action should the controller take?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-3-2 and 14 CFR 91.159(a), VFR-on-top cruising altitudes for magnetic courses 180-359 are EVEN altitudes plus 500 feet (e.g., 4,500, 6,500). At 5,500 feet (odd+500) on a 270-degree course, the aircraft is at an incorrect altitude. The controller must inform the pilot using the phraseology: 'VFR-ON-TOP CRUISING LEVELS FOR YOUR DIRECTION OF FLIGHT ARE: EVEN ALTITUDES PLUS FIVE HUNDRED FEET.'

choices.text: Inform the pilot that VFR-on-top cruising levels for a 270-degree course are even altitudes plus 500 feet.
No action; 5,500 feet is always an appropriate VFR cruising altitude.
Issue a safety alert because the altitude deviation creates an immediate collision hazard.
Instruct the aircraft to climb to FL180 where VFR-on-top cruising altitudes no longer apply.

Aircraft flying below 3,000 feet AGL at any time.
Aircraft in a holding pattern of 2 minutes or less, or turning.
All aircraft on IFR flight plans regardless of clearance type.

match.question_text: An aircraft on a visual approach executes a go-around at 200 feet AGL. The pilot expects to fly the published missed approach. Why is this expectation incorrect?

replacements.question_text: An aircraft on a visual approach executes a go-around at 200 feet AGL. The pilot expects to fly the published missed approach. Why is this expectation incorrect?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-1, a visual approach is not a standard instrument approach procedure and has no missed approach segment. A go-around from a visual approach must be handled as any go-around — appropriate IFR separation must be provided until the aircraft lands or the pilot cancels their IFR flight plan.

choices.text: Visual approaches use a different missed approach point than instrument approaches.
A visual approach has no missed approach segment; the go-around is handled as any go-around with IFR separation provided.
The published missed approach applies only when the ceiling is below 500 feet.

replacements.explanation: Per the 7-4-1 note, the pilot is responsible for their own terrain and obstruction avoidance during a go-around after conducting a visual approach. However, the facility can assign headings towards the lowest terrain and obstructions to assist.

Neither; terrain avoidance is automatically handled by the published missed approach segment.
Joint responsibility shared equally between ATC and the pilot.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-1a1, at airports with an operating control tower, what are the two options for directing an aircraft executing a go-around from a visual approach?

replacements.question_text: At airports with an operating control tower, what are the two options for directing an aircraft executing a go-around from a visual approach?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-1a, the two options are: (1) enter the traffic pattern for landing (no altitude assignment required; pilot climbs to pattern altitude and is responsible for terrain/obstruction avoidance), or (2) proceed as otherwise instructed by ATC (pilot complies with instructions; ATC is responsible to provide instructions for climb to minimum IFR altitude). Both require ATC to provide approved or visual separation from other IFR aircraft.

choices.text: Enter the traffic pattern for landing, or proceed as otherwise instructed by ATC.
Execute the published missed approach procedure, or request an IFR clearance back to the holding fix.
Climb to the MVA and hold, or cancel IFR and proceed VFR.
Return to the final approach fix and try again, or divert to the alternate airport.

choices.text: A full published missed approach procedure with holding instructions.
Only terrain and obstruction avoidance guidance.
Instructions to facilitate a climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations.
A complete new IFR clearance to an alternate airport.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-2, the minimum ceiling for a visual approach vector is 500 feet above the MVA/MIA. This is the specific numerical threshold that must be met to initiate the vector.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-2, at airports without weather reporting service, what must exist before a vector for a visual approach may be initiated?

replacements.question_text: At airports without weather reporting service, what must exist before a vector for a visual approach may be initiated?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-2, at airports without weather reporting, there must be reasonable assurance that descent and flight to the airport can be made visually and clear of clouds — for example, using area weather reports, PIREPs, pilot requests, etc.

choices.text: A PIREP from at least two other aircraft confirming conditions.
A METAR from the nearest airport within 30 NM.
Reasonable assurance that descent and flight to the airport can be made visually and clear of clouds.
A pilot report of the airport in sight before the vector is initiated.

replacements.question_text: What must the controller include in the phraseology when weather information is not available at the airport?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-2 phraseology, when weather information is not available, the controller includes 'WEATHER NOT AVAILABLE' with the vector clearance. This informs the pilot that no official weather is reported at the destination.

choices.text: USE CAUTION, NO WEATHER AVAILABLE.
WEATHER NOT AVAILABLE.
WEATHER UNKNOWN, PROCEED AT YOUR OWN RISK.

replacements.question_text: What authorizes towers to clear aircraft for visual approaches?
replacements.explanation: Per the 7-4-3 note, towers may exercise visual approach clearance authority when authorized by an LOA with the facility providing IFR service, or by a facility directive at collocated facilities.

choices.text: An LOA with the facility that provides the IFR service, or a facility directive at collocated facilities.
A blanket authorization in the facility SOP that applies to all towers regardless of facility type.
A verbal authorization from the TRACON supervisor on duty.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-3a3, at locations with an operating control tower, what happens when the pilot reports the airport in sight but not the preceding aircraft?

replacements.question_text: At locations with an operating control tower, what happens when the pilot reports the airport in sight but not the preceding aircraft?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-3a3, at towered airports when the pilot reports the airport or runway in sight but not the preceding aircraft, radar separation must be maintained until visual separation is provided — and visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.

choices.text: Radar separation must be maintained until visual separation is provided, and visual separation is not authorized when the lead aircraft is a super.
The approach clearance is withheld until the pilot reports both the airport and the preceding aircraft in sight.
The controller may issue the visual approach clearance immediately because the airport being in sight is sufficient.

match.question_text: Complete: Under 7-4-4b1, when conducting visual approaches to multiple runways, do not permit the respective aircraft flight paths to intersect unless approved separation is maintained. Ensure the intercept angle to the extended runway centerline is not greater than _____ degrees.

replacements.question_text: Complete: when conducting visual approaches to multiple runways, do not permit the respective aircraft flight paths to intersect unless approved separation is maintained. Ensure the intercept angle to the extended runway centerline is not greater than _____ degrees.
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-4b1, the intercept angle to the extended runway centerline must not exceed 30 degrees. This is to reduce the potential for overshoots and preclude side-by-side operations with aircraft in a bank toward one another.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-4b2, when must approved separation be ensured during approaches to multiple runways?

replacements.question_text: When must approved separation be ensured during approaches to multiple runways?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-4b2, approved separation must be ensured until the aircraft assigned the higher altitude has descended through the altitude assigned to the lower aircraft, when the visual approach flight paths intersect.

choices.text: Until both aircraft report the runway environment in sight.
Until one pilot reports the other aircraft in sight and accepts visual separation.
Until the aircraft assigned the higher altitude has descended through the altitude assigned to the lower aircraft.
Until both aircraft have crossed the runway threshold.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-5, which of the following is NOT a condition for clearing an aircraft for a Charted Visual Flight Procedure (CVFP)?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a condition for clearing an aircraft for a Charted Visual Flight Procedure (CVFP)?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-5, conditions are: operating control tower, published name of CVFP and runway in clearance, ceiling at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA (or if following another aircraft on the approach, the ceiling must be at or above the MVA), and the pilot affirms charted visual landmark or preceding aircraft in sight. A non-towered airport is not permitted for CVFP operations.

choices.text: An operating control tower is required.
The reported ceiling must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA.
The published name of the CVFP and the landing runway must be in the approach clearance.
The pilot must have at least two prior landings at the airport in the last 90 days.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-6, what must the reported ceiling be at the airport of intended landing before issuing an RVFP approach clearance?

replacements.question_text: What must the reported ceiling be at the airport of intended landing before issuing an RVFP approach clearance?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-6c, the reported ceiling at the airport must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA and the visibility must be 3 miles or greater. This is the same weather threshold as vectoring for a visual approach under 7-4-2.

choices.text: At least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA with visibility 3 miles or greater.
At least the published circling minimums for the approach being substituted.
At least 1,000 feet above the MVA with visibility 5 miles or greater.
At or above basic VFR minimums for the airspace classification.

Authorized operators only, as determined by facility RVFP LOAs.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-7, what is the pilot responsible for maintaining during a contact approach?

replacements.question_text: What is the pilot responsible for maintaining during a contact approach?
replacements.explanation: Per the 7-4-7 note, the pilot is responsible for maintaining required flight visibility, cloud clearance, and terrain/obstruction clearance. Unless otherwise restricted, the pilot may deviate from the prescribed IFR pattern to proceed to the destination airport.

choices.text: Only terrain and obstruction clearance; ATC maintains cloud separation.
Only flight visibility; the published approach ensures cloud and terrain clearance.
Flight visibility, cloud clearance, and terrain/obstruction clearance.

match.question_text: Under 7-4-7, what reported ground visibility is required before a contact approach may be authorized?

replacements.question_text: What reported ground visibility is required before a contact approach may be authorized?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-4-7b, the reported ground visibility at the destination airport must be at least 1 statute mile. This is lower than the 3-mile requirement for visual approach vectors. If ground visibility is not available, flight visibility must be at least 1 statute mile.

choices.text: At least 3 statute miles.
At least 5 statute miles.
At least 1 statute mile.

replacements.question_text: Complete: SVFR operations in weather conditions less than basic VFR minima are authorized in the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class _____ airspace.
replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-1a, SVFR is authorized in the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, and Class E airspace. Class A airspace is excluded because SVFR is a VFR operation and VFR is prohibited in Class A (per 7-1-1).

match.question_text: Under 7-5-1b, what requirement must be met at an airport with an operating control tower before a fixed-wing SVFR clearance may be issued?

replacements.question_text: What requirement must be met at an airport with an operating control tower before a fixed-wing SVFR clearance may be issued?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-1b, at locations with an operating control tower, the ground visibility must be at least 1 statute mile before a fixed-wing SVFR clearance can be issued. Helicopters are exempt from this minimum and may operate SVFR in less than 1 mile visibility.

choices.text: The pilot must have filed an SVFR flight plan at least 30 minutes before departure.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-2a, SVFR has lower priority than IFR. The rule is: SVFR may be approved only if IFR aircraft are not delayed. If an SVFR clearance would delay an IFR arrival or departure, the SVFR request must be denied.

match.question_text: Under 7-5-2b, if SVFR cannot be granted because of IFR traffic departing every 2 minutes, what must the controller tell the SVFR pilot?

replacements.question_text: If SVFR cannot be granted because of IFR traffic departing every 2 minutes, what must the controller tell the SVFR pilot?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-2b, when SVFR cannot be granted due to IFR traffic (departing every 2 minutes or arriving on final with the same interval), the controller must advise the pilot of the expected delay using: 'EXPECT (time) DELAY.' This is a precise phraseology requirement.

choices.text: SVFR NOT AUTHORIZED, ADVISE WHEN READY TO FILE IFR.
EXPECT (time) DELAY.
UNABLE SVFR DUE TO IFR TRAFFIC, STAND BY.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-3a, nonradar or visual separation must be applied between: SVFR fixed-wing aircraft, SVFR fixed-wing and SVFR helicopters, and SVFR fixed-wing and IFR aircraft. Note that radar separation is excluded from SVFR fixed-wing operations.

choices.text: Nonradar or visual separation.

Visual separation only; nonradar is not applicable.

match.question_text: Under 7-5-4, what is the minimum vertical buffer between an SVFR aircraft and conflicting IFR traffic?

replacements.question_text: What is the minimum vertical buffer between an SVFR aircraft and conflicting IFR traffic?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-4, the controller clears the SVFR aircraft at or below an altitude that is at least 500 feet below any conflicting IFR traffic, but not below the MSA. This 500-foot buffer provides vertical separation while allowing the SVFR pilot cloud-clearance flexibility.

choices.text: At least 1,000 feet below conflicting IFR traffic.
At least 300 feet below conflicting IFR traffic.

match.question_text: When applying vertical separation between an SVFR aircraft and IFR traffic, why is the SVFR aircraft not assigned a fixed altitude to maintain?

replacements.question_text: When applying vertical separation between an SVFR aircraft and IFR traffic, why is the SVFR aircraft not assigned a fixed altitude to maintain?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-4, SVFR aircraft must remain clear of clouds. A fixed altitude could force the aircraft into a cloud layer. Instead, the controller clears the SVFR aircraft at or below an altitude at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic but not below the MSA, giving the pilot flexibility to maneuver around clouds.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-5a, local SVFR operations may be authorized for a specified period if the aircraft can be recalled when traffic or weather conditions require. replacements.question_text: May a helicopter conduct SVFR operations when ground visibility is below 1 mile?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-7, 14 CFR part 91 does not prohibit helicopter SVFR flight when visibility is less than 1 mile. This is a key difference from fixed-wing SVFR operations, which require at least 1 mile ground visibility at towered airports (7-5-1b).

choices.text: No, helicopters require the same 1-mile visibility as fixed-wing aircraft.
Yes, 14 CFR part 91 does not prohibit helicopter SVFR flight when visibility is less than 1 mile.

replacements.question_text: What must the controller do for an arriving aircraft operating outside Class B, C, D, or E surface area when the pilot advises flight visibility is less than 1 mile?
replacements.explanation: Per 7-5-8, for an arriving aircraft outside Class B/C/D/E surface area, if the pilot advises flight visibility is less than 1 mile, the controller tells the pilot to remain clear of clouds and of the airport traffic area. This advisory is necessary because SVFR minimums cannot be met, but the aircraft is outside controlled airspace surface areas.

Tell the pilot to land immediately at the nearest suitable airport.

replacements.explanation: Per 7-7-3, separation may be by any one of: visual separation (per 7-2-1), radar separation (per Chapter 5), nonradar separation (per Chapter 6), or pilot-applied visual separation (per 7-2-1). This gives controllers flexibility in TRSA separation methods.

match.question_text: A trainee says that pilot estimates and pilot progress reports serve the same function as control estimates under 8-1-8. How should you correct this misconception?

replacements.question_text: A trainee says that pilot estimates and pilot progress reports serve the same function as control estimates. How should you correct this misconception?
replacements.explanation: Per 8-1-8, pilot estimates and progress reports are inputs the controller may use to calculate control estimates. They are not the same thing as control estimates — control estimates are the controller's or automation's time-referenced position determination.

choices.text: They are identical — the paragraph treats pilot reports and control estimates as interchangeable.
Pilot estimates override control estimates and must be used instead.
Pilot estimates and progress reports are data sources used to calculate control estimates; they are not the same thing as the control estimate itself.
Control estimates may never use pilot-provided data.

replacements.explanation: Per 9-1-3b, controllers must avoid changes in the route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan. Traffic conflicts must be resolved by moving other aircraft, not by altering the flight check aircraft's profile.

choices.text: Yes, but only if the pilot agrees to the altitude change before it is issued.
No. Avoid changes in route or altitude from the pilot's filed initial flight plan.
Yes, traffic conflict resolution takes priority over flight check protection.
No, but only because altitude changes always void the flight check data.

match.question_text: A special radar accuracy flight check aircraft has been assigned discrete beacon code 0123. A supervisor suggests changing the code to resolve a duplication. What is the correct controller action?

replacements.question_text: A special radar accuracy flight check aircraft has been assigned discrete beacon code 0123. A supervisor suggests changing the code to resolve a duplication. What is the correct controller action?
replacements.explanation: Per 9-1-3d, do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft. Changing the code could disrupt the calibration data being collected.

choices.text: Change the code and advise the pilot of the new assignment.
Coordinate with the flight check aircraft to determine an acceptable new code.
Do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code.
Change the code only if the duplication is with an aircraft at the same altitude.

match.question_text: Under 9-1-3, a controller is working a flight check aircraft. She wants to give the aircraft a 5-minute delay for sequencing. No emergency exists. Is this permitted?

replacements.question_text: A controller is working a flight check aircraft. She wants to give the aircraft a 5-minute delay for sequencing. No emergency exists. Is this permitted?
replacements.explanation: Per 9-1-3c, 'Do not impose air traffic control delays in the flight except to preclude emergency situations.' A sequencing delay does not meet the emergency exception.

choices.text: Yes, short delays of 5 minutes or less are permitted for operational sequencing.
Yes, as long as the pilot agrees to the delay before it is imposed.
No, ATC delays may not be imposed except to preclude an emergency situation.
No, but only because the delay exceeds 2 minutes; delays under 2 minutes are permissible.

match.question_text: Under 9-1-3, a special radar accuracy flight check aircraft has been assigned discrete beacon code 0123. A supervisor suggests reassigning a new code to avoid duplication with another aircraft. No emergency exists. Is this permitted?

replacements.question_text: A special radar accuracy flight check aircraft has been assigned discrete beacon code 0123. A supervisor suggests reassigning a new code to avoid duplication with another aircraft. No emergency exists. Is this permitted?
replacements.explanation: Per 9-1-3d, 'Do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft.' Changing the code for non-emergency reasons is prohibited even if it would be operationally convenient.

choices.text: Yes, as long as the new code is coordinated with the pilot first.
Yes, beacon code management for duplication is a routine ATC function that overrides this restriction.
Yes, but only if the aircraft has already completed its inspection run.
No, do not change the previously assigned discrete beacon code of special radar accuracy flight check aircraft.

A device containing no dangerous materials but closely resembling nuclear or explosive items classified as dangerous, which could be easily mistaken for dangerous counterparts.

The reason for refusal and an estimated time the pilot can accept the amendment.

replacements.question_text: What is the difference in termination procedure for an SFRA aircraft that is landing inside the area versus one that is not?
replacements.explanation: Per 9-2-10c1-2, non-landing aircraft are told to squawk VFR with frequency change approved upon exit. Landing aircraft are instructed to remain on the assigned transponder code until after landing.

choices.text: Non-landing: squawk VFR, frequency change approved upon exit. Landing: remain on assigned transponder code until after landing.
Both get the same termination: squawk VFR, frequency change approved.
Non-landing gets handoff to next sector; landing aircraft terminates with the tower.
Landing aircraft squawks VFR on short final; non-landing keeps the code until 10 NM outside the SFRA.

replacements.question_text: What is the relationship between ATC security tracking and basic separation services in the DC SFRA?
replacements.explanation: Per 9-2-10, ATC security tracking is a distinct function from basic separation services. IFR, SVFR, Class B, Class C, and TRSA separation services do not apply to security tracking. Security tracking is a surveillance and reporting function, not a separation function.

choices.text: ATC security tracking replaces all normal separation services for aircraft under tracking.
Basic separation services (IFR, SVFR, Class B, Class C, TRSA) do not apply to ATC security tracking.
Security tracking uses the same separation minima as Class B airspace services.
Only IFR separation services apply during security tracking; VFR services are suspended.

replacements.question_text: A pilot reports unusual lights that the controller cannot see on radar. Is the controller obligated to inform the supervisor?
replacements.explanation: Yes. 9-8-1 applies to any reported OR observed activity. A pilot report triggers the obligation regardless of whether the controller can independently verify it on radar.

choices.text: No, because the controller must have independent radar confirmation before reporting.
Yes, because 9-8-1 applies to any reported UAP or unexplained phenomena activity.
No, because UAP reports only apply to objects that are visually confirmed by ATC personnel.
Yes, but only if the pilot declares an emergency in connection with the sighting.

replacements.explanation: Per 9-8-1, the obligation is triggered by any reported OR observed UAP/unexplained phenomena activity. The controller does not need to personally observe it — a report from a pilot or another source is sufficient.

choices.text: It must be personally observed by the controller on radar or visually.
It must be confirmed by both a pilot report AND radar observation.
It must be reported by at least three independent sources to be actionable.
It must be either reported (by pilots or others) or observed (by the controller or facility). Either source triggers the obligation.

content.situation: A controller encounters an unusual traffic scenario not described anywhere in FAA Order JO 7110.65. A co-worker suggests the controller must stop providing service entirely until a supervisor writes a local procedure.
content.question_text: What does FAA Order JO 7110.65 require the controller to do when a situation is not covered by the order?

choices.text: Refuse to provide any further service until a new procedure is drafted and approved.
Exercise best judgment to handle the situation while continuing to provide service.
Transfer all affected aircraft to another frequency immediately.
Apply whichever procedure feels most similar, even if it contradicts the order.

content.explanation: Per 1-1-1, controllers exercise their best judgment when they encounter situations not covered by the order. They are not required to cease operations or apply contradictory procedures.

content.situation: Two civilian facilities have been using joint arrival procedures based on a verbal understanding. One supervisor wants to formalize the arrangement with reduced separation minima below the order's values, arguing that a signed LOA between the two facilities is sufficient to authorize the reduction.
content.question_text: Can two civilian facilities use an LOA to authorize separation minima below those in FAA Order JO 7110.65?

choices.text: Yes; any two facilities may agree to lower minima as long as the agreement is documented in an LOA.
Yes, but only if the LOA is also approved by the service area office.
No; LOAs may never address minima — that is reserved for waivers under JO 7210.3.
No; LOAs only supplement the order and may not specify minima less than the order's except when appropriate military authority authorizes reduced separation between military aircraft.

content.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order and may not specify minima less than the order's minima unless appropriate military authority has authorized reduced separation between military aircraft. Civilian facilities cannot self-authorize lower minima through an LOA.

content.situation: An Air Force unit wants USAF-specific procedures that differ from the order for operations involving only military aircraft. The request is routed directly to AJV-P without coordination through the appropriate Air Force headquarters.
content.question_text: Is this routing correct for USAF-only procedural deviations?

choices.text: Yes; the local Air Force unit can approve its own deviations without higher headquarters coordination.
Yes; as long as AJV-P receives the request, military chain-of-command review is optional.
No; only the Department of the Army can approve procedures for any military aircraft.
No; military operations require prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters, and for USAF-only procedures HQ AFFSA is the approval authority.

content.explanation: Per 1-1-11b, prior military headquarters approval is required. The Terminal note specifies HQ AFFSA as the approval authority for USAF procedures or minima that differ from the order and involve military aircraft only.

content.situation: Your facility is physically located in the Western-Pacific region but its service area office is in the Northwest Mountain region. A reference in the order requires contacting a Flight Standards regional office. A coworker suggests calling the Northwest Mountain region office because that is where the service area office sits.
content.question_text: Which region should the facility contact?

choices.text: The Northwest Mountain region, because service area offices always coordinate regional matters.
The Western-Pacific region, where the facility is physically located.
Either region works; physical and service-area regions are interchangeable.
Washington headquarters, because the order does not specify which region to use.

content.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the facility contacts the FAA region where the facility is physically located — not the region where the service area office is located. Physical location governs.
content.para_context: REFERENCES TO FAA NON-AIR TRAFFIC ORGANIZATIONS

content.situation: A controller reads a paragraph that says controllers 'will issue a clearance' and interprets 'will' as mandatory, meaning the clearance must be issued in all cases. A supervisor agrees. The controller re-reads the word meanings section and sees that 'will' means futurity, not a requirement.
content.question_text: Does 'will' in FAA Order JO 7110.65 mean a procedure is mandatory?

choices.text: Yes; 'will' and 'must' are interchangeable in this order.
Yes; 'will' always means mandatory in FAA publications.
No; 'will' means futurity, not a requirement. 'Must' is the term that means mandatory.
It depends on whether the section is annotated TERMINAL.

content.explanation: Per 1-2-1e, 'will' means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure. This is one of the most commonly misunderstood definitions in the order. 'Must' is the term that indicates a mandatory procedure.

content.situation: Two aircraft are on courses whose protected airspaces intersect. The angular difference between their courses is approximately 30 degrees. A trainee wants to classify them as crossing courses because the paths intersect.
content.question_text: What is the correct course classification for intersecting protected airspaces with a 30-degree angular difference?

choices.text: Crossing courses, because any intersecting paths are crossing courses.
Same courses, because the angular difference is less than 45 degrees and the protected airspaces intersect.
Opposite/reciprocal courses, because the angle is too small to be crossing.
No classification applies because the protected airspaces also need to overlap in a specific way.

content.explanation: Per 1-2-2a, same courses are courses whose protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect and whose angular difference is less than 45 degrees. Intersection plus < 45 degrees satisfies both conditions for same courses.

content.situation: A paragraph annotated USAF describes a military procedure. An Army controller at a joint-use facility reads it and assumes it also applies to Army aircraft since they are also military.
content.question_text: Does a paragraph annotated USAF apply to Army aircraft?

choices.text: Yes; all military annotations apply to any U.S. military service.
No; USAF annotation means the procedure applies only to the U.S. Air Force.
Yes, but only at joint-use facilities.
It depends on whether the Army facility has a letter of agreement with the Air Force unit.

content.explanation: Per 1-2-5e, USAF denotes the procedure applies only to the U.S. Air Force. USN applies only to Navy, and USA only to Army. Each service annotation is exclusive to that service.

content.situation: A pilot transmits 'Center, N345AB, we've lost pressurization and need to descend, over.' The pilot never says 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan,' but the aircraft is now descending without a clearance and you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists. Another controller says the transmission cannot be treated as an emergency because the pilot used neither emergency word.
content.para_context: If 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency. Because of the infinite variety of emergencies, specific procedures cannot be prescribed; select the most appropriate course of action.
content.question_text: How should this situation be handled?

choices.text: Wait for the pilot to use 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' before applying emergency procedures, because only the pilot can determine whether an emergency exists.
Handle it as an emergency and take the most appropriate course of action under the circumstances.
Treat it as a routine descent request because the loss of pressurization was not described as an emergency.
Issue a Brasher warning for the unauthorized descent and do not apply emergency handling.

content.explanation: Per 10-1-1c, if the emergency words are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency.

content.situation: You are working an aircraft in distress and a nearby VFR aircraft offers to escort it to the nearest airport. The escort pilot states they have never flown formation and cannot see the distressed aircraft. Both aircraft are in instrument conditions.
content.para_context: When considering an escort, evaluate the close formation required and give special consideration if the maneuver goes through clouds. Do not allow aircraft to join up in formation during emergency conditions unless the pilots are familiar with and capable of formation flight, and they can communicate and have visual contact with each other.
content.question_text: Should the escort be allowed?

choices.text: No, because the VFR pilot is not familiar with formation flight, lacks visual contact, and the maneuver would go through clouds.
Yes, because any available aircraft should be used to escort an aircraft in distress.
Yes, but only if the distressed pilot explicitly requests the escort.
No, only because the escorting aircraft is operating VFR and cannot join an emergency operation.

content.explanation: Per 10-1-4f, formation joining is not allowed during emergencies unless both pilots are familiar with and capable of formation flight AND they can communicate and have visual contact. Under 10-1-4e, special consideration applies if the maneuver goes through clouds.

content.instruction: Which notification path is incorrect for this Chapter 10 scenario?
content.question_text: A terminal facility hears an ELT signal from an aircraft. Which of the following is NOT the correct notification action?
content.explanation: Under 10-2-10b, terminal facilities notify the ARTCC which coordinates with the RCC. Under 10-2-10c, they attempt to obtain fixes or bearings. Under 10-2-10e, they forward fixes/bearings to the ARTCC. Direct notification to the RCC is for en route facilities only (10-2-10a).

choices.text: Notify the ARTCC, which will coordinate with the RCC.
Notify the RCC directly, as terminal facilities are authorized to bypass the ARTCC for time-critical ELT events.
Attempt to obtain fixes or bearings on the ELT signal.
Forward any fixes or bearings and other pertinent information to the ARTCC.

content.situation: At 1410Z, a pilot reports a green laser from the northwest at 2,500 feet. At 1425Z, a second pilot reports a green laser from the northwest at 3,000 feet. It is now 1435Z with no further reports. You are at a terminal facility.
content.explanation: Per 10-2-14a, frequency broadcasts occur every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the LAST report. The last report was at 1425Z, so broadcasts continue until 1445Z. Per 10-2-14b, the terminal ATIS must include the laser event for one hour following the last report — until 1525Z — with time, location, altitude, color, and direction as reported.

choices.text: Continue frequency broadcasts every 5 minutes until 1445Z and include the event on the ATIS until 1525Z.
Stop all broadcasts at 1430Z because the first report was 20 minutes ago.
Broadcast only until 1425Z since that was the last report and the rule runs from each individual report.
Include the event on the ATIS until 1510Z because the one-hour clock starts from the FIRST report.

content.instruction: Choose the required controller response to the volcanic ash encounter.
content.situation: A B737 reports entering a volcanic ash cloud at FL350, with engine vibration increasing. The pilot says 'we are in distress.' Another controller suggests issuing an immediate climb to FL390 to get above the ash and a turn toward the nearest airport without waiting for pilot concurrence.
content.explanation: Per 10-2-18b: consider the aircraft in an emergency (b1), do NOT initiate climb clearances to turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft has exited the ash cloud (b2), and do NOT attempt to provide escape vectors without pilot concurrence (b3). Both suggested actions are prohibited. The recommended escape maneuver per the note is to reverse course and begin a descent if terrain permits, but it remains the pilot's responsibility to determine the safest escape route.

choices.text: Treat as an emergency. Do not issue a climb clearance. Do not provide escape vectors unless the pilot concurs.
Issue the climb immediately because getting above the ash cloud is the priority for turbine aircraft.
Issue both the climb and the turn because the pilot's distress declaration authorizes the controller to choose the escape route.
Do not treat it as an emergency unless the pilot uses the word 'Mayday' specifically.

content.instruction: Choose the required response to the VFR pilot requesting radar assistance.
content.situation: A VFR pilot in deteriorating weather says the aircraft has full IFR instrumentation but the pilot has not maintained instrument currency and refuses to declare an emergency. The pilot asks for vectors to the nearest airport. A nearby airport is reporting 800 overcast and 2 miles visibility — IFR conditions.
content.explanation: The pilot is not IFR qualified (currency lapsed), so the IFR-clearance path does not apply. Per 10-2-8c1, inform the pilot of airports where VFR conditions are reported and provide other pertinent weather. If no VFR airport is feasible or the pilot declines, per 10-2-8c2, radar assistance may still be provided if the pilot refuses to declare an emergency AND the controller determines the exact nature of the radar services desired. The pilot asking for 'vectors to the nearest airport' is a specific service request that meets this threshold.

choices.text: Inform the pilot of any airports with VFR conditions. If none are feasible or the pilot declines, provide radar vectors since the pilot has specified the exact service desired (vectors to nearest airport).
Declare an emergency on the pilot's behalf and issue an IFR clearance, because the aircraft is IFR-equipped and safety requires it.
Refuse assistance because the pilot is not IFR qualified and refuses to declare an emergency.
Issue an IFR clearance without a flight plan since the pilot has already entered IFR conditions and terrain clearance is at risk.

content.para_context: RADAR ASSISTANCE TO VFR AIRCRAFT IN WEATHER DIFFICULTY

content.question_text: How should you plan control actions for this aircraft's altitude profile?

choices.text: Plan for the aircraft to hold the last assigned altitude all the way to Winnipeg, because all radio-failure aircraft maintain clearance-limit altitude to destination.
Plan only for the 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit — the border-crossing rule does not apply because the destination is in Canada, not the U.S.
Plan for immediate climb to filed altitude after the clearance limit because the terrain is flat and there is no safety concern.

Plan for the aircraft to maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever higher) until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceed at filed altitudes. Because U.S. airspace is within 10 minutes of the clearance limit, the climb to the border-crossing altitude will commence at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.

content.explanation: Per 12-1-5: (1) maintain last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever higher) until 10 min beyond clearance limit, then filed altitudes; (2) if U.S. airspace is within 10 min after the clearance limit, climb to border-crossing altitude commences at the estimated time of crossing the boundary. Both rules apply here — the destination being in Canada does not negate the border-crossing rule when the route transits U.S. airspace.

content.explanation: Per the note to 13-1-1, the current plan for EDST use is what EAS predicts the aircraft will fly, and it may include clearances not yet issued to the aircraft. Unissued planned clearances are part of the modeled trajectory.

content.question_text: A controller enters a planned route amendment into the automation but has not yet issued the clearance to the pilot. Is this amendment part of the current plan for EDST purposes?

choices.text: Yes. The current plan is what EAS predicts the aircraft will fly and may include clearances that have not yet been issued to the aircraft.
No. Unissued clearances are in a separate pending plan and are excluded from EDST trajectory modeling.
No. Only clearances the pilot has acknowledged on frequency are part of the current plan.
Yes, but only if the amendment was entered more than five minutes before the aircraft reaches the amended route segment.

content.explanation: Per the note to 13-1-1, the current plan for EDST use is what EAS predicts the aircraft will fly, and it may include clearances not yet issued to the aircraft. Unissued planned clearances are part of the modeled trajectory.

content.situation: You are working a sector with EDST operational. A trainee wants to use the EDST trajectory display to provide radar separation between two converging aircraft at the same altitude, arguing that the predicted trajectories show them diverging before loss of separation. Another controller says EDST should not be used for current separation at all.
content.question_text: What is the appropriate role of EDST in this situation?

choices.text: Use EDST trajectory predictions as the primary basis for radar separation when the automated separation appears satisfied on the display.
Rely on EDST for separation because EDST is designed to replace the radar display for tactical control.

Use EDST for strategic planning and conflict prediction, but maintain radar separation using actual radar track data. EDST is a strategic planning tool that uses current plans which may include unissued clearances.
Ignore EDST entirely because it is only for historical analysis and post-event review.

content.explanation: EDST is defined as an integrated ERAM function used for strategic planning responsibilities. It predicts conflicts using current plan data which may include clearances not yet issued to the aircraft. EDST is not a replacement for radar separation—controllers must maintain separation using actual radar track data while using EDST for planning and conflict prediction purposes.

content.situation: Your sector team has been busy and an aircraft's interim altitude change has not been entered in the automation. The aircraft is level at an intermediate altitude waiting for further climb. Another controller says the entry can wait because the aircraft is showing level on radar, and the next sector will enter it on handoff. The sector has no facility directive authorizing an exception for interim altitudes.
content.question_text: What is required regarding the interim altitude entry?

choices.text: The entry can wait until the aircraft receives its final climb clearance, since interim altitudes are only relevant at the final cruising level.
The entry is optional because the aircraft is level on radar and the automation will self-correct the trajectory model.
The next sector will enter it on handoff, so the current sector has no responsibility to update the interim altitude.
The sector team must enter the interim altitude now. Conflict probe accuracy in assigning alert notification depends on the entry, and no facility directive authorizes an exception.

content.explanation: The sector team must perform automation entries in a timely manner. Conflict probe accuracy in assigning alert notification is dependent upon entry of a flight's interim altitude. An exception to this requirement may be authorized only for certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive—workload alone does not authorize skipping the entry.

content.situation: A flight is in holding using the ERAM Hold View. When the aircraft is cleared out of hold, another controller assumes the delay was manually recorded on a strip and does not verify whether the Hold View auto-recording captured it. A third controller says delay recording is optional because the facility has no specific TMU request for this flight's delay data.
content.question_text: How should delay information be handled for this holding aircraft?

choices.text: Skip delay recording entirely for this flight since TMU has not specifically requested the information.
Manually record the delay on a strip because Hold View auto-recording cannot be relied upon for official delay reporting.
Record the delay only if the total holding time exceeds 10 minutes, because shorter delays are not operationally significant.

Recognize that Hold View automatically records the delay when the aircraft is cleared out of hold, and ensure facility delay reporting directives are followed regardless of whether TMU requests the data.

content.explanation: Delay information must be recorded per applicable delay reporting directives. When using the ERAM Hold View, delays are automatically recorded when the aircraft is cleared out of hold. Delay recording is mandatory regardless of whether TMU has specifically requested the data for that flight.

content.situation: Current forecast wind data are unavailable. One controller wants to suspend all conflict probe and trial planning immediately, saying the tools are unreliable without wind data. Another wants to treat all alerts as fully accurate, because if the conflict probe generated an alert it must still be valid even without wind data.
content.question_text: What is the correct approach?

choices.text: Suspend both tools until forecast wind data are restored, because neither can function reliably without wind data.
Continue use of conflict probe and trial planning, but recognize that the unavailability of current forecast wind data means alert and trajectory data may be affected.
Continue using both tools with no special recognition, because forecast wind loss does not affect automated alerting or trajectory predictions.
Use trial planning only and suspend conflict probe, because trial planning calculations do not depend on wind data.

content.explanation: 13-1-14 Requires continued use of both tools with the explicit caveat that alert and trajectory data may be affected. Neither suspension nor unqualified use is correct.

content.question_text: How does the operational response to loss of interfacility connectivity compare to the response to loss of forecast winds?

choices.text: Forecast wind loss requires complete suspension of tools; connectivity loss allows continued use without any special recognition.
Both require immediate suspension of all EDST functions until the data source is restored.
Both require continued use of the affected tools with recognition that certain data may be affected—but the specific data that may be affected differs between the two situations.
There is no operational difference between the two situations.

content.explanation: Both forecast wind loss and interfacility connectivity loss follow a 'continue use with recognition' pattern—neither requires suspending EDST. However, the affected data differs: wind loss may affect both alert and trajectory data, while connectivity loss specifically may affect alert data. Controllers must understand this distinction.

content.question_text: During a surveillance outage, electronic flight data remains available. A controller wants to use it for radar-based separation because the data appears current on the display. Is this authorized?

choices.text: Yes. During an outage, electronic flight data may be used for any purpose that maintains traffic flow, including radar separation, because the normal operational limitations are suspended.
No. Electronic flight data during an outage is authorized only to support situational awareness while the facility transitions, not as a replacement for radar separation.
Yes, but only after the operations supervisor approves the expanded use in writing.
No. Electronic flight data must be shut down completely during any surveillance outage to prevent automation conflicts.

content.explanation: 13-1-16 Limits electronic flight data use during an outage to supporting situational awareness while transitioning to alternate automation or nonradar procedures. It does not authorize using it as a substitute for radar separation.

content.situation: Your sector is assigned, by facility directive, responsibility for updating Airspace Configuration Elements. A controller activates an SAA in the Airspace Status View and announces that this completes the airspace activation coordination with the using agency. The controller also updates an Airport Stream Filter to inactive status but does not notify the operations supervisor, saying the status change is already visible in automation so no additional notification is needed.
content.question_text: What duty requirements have been misunderstood?

choices.text: Both requirements were handled correctly. SAA activation in the Status View always satisfies coordination needs, and the supervisor can see all automation status changes without being notified.

Airspace Status View activation does not by itself constitute coordination for airspace activation unless covered by an LOA or facility directive. Additionally, the operations supervisor must be notified when the status of an Airspace Configuration Element has been modified.
Only the SAA coordination requirement was misunderstood. The supervisor notification is optional when the change is made through automation since the change is automatically logged.
Only the supervisor notification was missed. SAA activation in the Airspace Status View always serves as coordination for airspace activation under any circumstances.

content.explanation: 13-1-17 Note: Airspace Status View activation alone is NOT coordination unless an LOA or facility directive says otherwise. 13-1-17(c): for elements designated as sector responsibility, the operations supervisor must be notified when status is modified. Both were violated.

content.question_text: A sector team has activated Stop Probe for an aircraft that will be handed off to a non-EDST tower facility. What is required before transfer of control?

choices.text: Coordinate the Stop Probe status with the tower supervisor because towers cannot see conflict probe status without verbal notification.
Restart conflict probe before the handoff, because Stop Probe must always be restarted before any transfer of control.
Cancel the flight plan and re-file it as a VFR flight to avoid the Stop Probe restriction.
Nothing additional regarding Stop Probe restart. The requirement to restart conflict probe before transfer does not apply to aircraft entering a non-EDST facility.

content.explanation: The note to 13-1-2(e) explicitly exempts aircraft entering a non-EDST facility from the Stop Probe restart requirement before transfer of control.

content.situation: A conflict probe alert fires for two aircraft at FL330. One aircraft is an A380 super heavy. The alert shows separation will be the standard 5 NM minimum. A controller says the alert is sufficient because conflict probe accounts for all separation requirements, so no additional buffer is needed.
content.question_text: How should the A380 alert be evaluated?

choices.text: Treat the alert the same as any other because conflict probe is calibrated for all aircraft types including A380 operations.
Suppress the alert because A380 separation requirements are handled by a separate automation system, not conflict probe.

Evaluate the alert recognizing that conflict probe does not account for instances where greater separation may be needed, such as for A380 operations, and ensure appropriate wake turbulence or super-heavy separation is applied.
Rely on the alert and reduce separation to the minimum shown because conflict probe guarantees standard separation is sufficient for all aircraft.

content.explanation: Per the note to 13-1-2, conflict probe alerts are based on approved radar separation but do not account for instances where greater separation may be needed, including A380 operations. The controller must apply additional separation requirements beyond what the alert shows.

content.situation: A pilot requests direct to the destination airport. EDST is operational and sector workload is moderate. Another controller says trial planning should only be used for conflict resolution, not for user requests. A third controller says trial planning may be used for user requests but the sector must be completely quiet before using it.
content.question_text: Under what conditions may trial planning be used to evaluate this pilot request?

choices.text: Do not use trial planning at all because user requests must be evaluated based on controller judgment without automation assistance.
Wait until all conflicts are resolved because trial planning may only be used after all alerts have been cleared from the sector.
Use trial planning now because EDST is operational and sector priorities permit. Trial planning is specifically authorized for evaluating the feasibility of granting user requests.
Use trial planning only if the pilot declares an emergency, because otherwise user requests are not within the scope of trial planning.

content.explanation: 13-1-3(B) explicitly lists the feasibility of granting user requests as one of the four uses for trial planning when EDST is operational and sector priorities permit.

content.situation: Your sector is working a mix of routine and special flights: two air refueling missions, a formation flight, three aircraft to be sequenced for the arrival meter, and several standard overflights. A trainee has left all flights in a single ACL list and says the SPA is only for emergencies. The trainee also has a nonradar flight in the sector with no strip posted.
content.question_text: How should the sector team manage these flights?

choices.text: Move all aircraft to the SPA so they are consolidated in one place regardless of significance, and skip strips for nonradar flights because the ACL already tracks them.
Leave all flights in the standard ACL because the SPA is reserved for emergency aircraft only, and nonradar flights do not require strips when EDST is operational.
Group the air refueling missions, formation, and sequencing aircraft in the SPA because they have special significance, and post a flight progress strip for the nonradar flight.
Use the SPA only for the formation flight and ignore the others, and use an electronic-only notation for the nonradar flight since paper strips are no longer required.

content.explanation: 13-1-5(E) says the SPA should be used to group aircraft that have special significance, with air refueling missions, formations, and aircraft to be sequenced as listed examples. 13-1-5(f) requires posting strips for nonradar flights.
content.para_context: THE AIRCRAFT LIST (ACL), DEPARTURE LIST (DL) AND FLIGHT DATA
