content.situation: You must coordinate a flight with a VFR tower that has no automated coordination capability. Your team uses the Coordination Menu to annotate the manual coordination. Later, the flight's route is amended. A trainee wants to leave the old yellow Coordination Indicator in place, saying it provides useful history for the next controller.
content.question_text: How should the Coordination Indicator be managed after the flight plan is changed?

choices.text: Leave the yellow coding permanently because it documents the prior manual coordination that occurred.
Remove the yellow coding immediately when the change is entered to keep the display clean.
Remove the yellow coding from the Coordination Indicator after any appropriate action has been taken regarding the flight plan change.
Change the yellow coding to a different color to indicate the coordination has been superseded.

content.explanation: 13-1-6(B) requires removing the yellow coding after any appropriate action has been taken when the flight plan is subsequently changed. The coding should not remain as history, nor be removed before action is complete.

content.question_text: The first sector displaying ERT coding must issue and send/acknowledge the route. When must this be accomplished relative to handoff?

choices.text: After the hand-off has been initiated and accepted by the receiving sector.
At any time before the aircraft physically crosses the sector boundary.
Prior to initiating a hand-off, unless verbally coordinated or as specified in appropriate facility directives.

ERT coding must not be sent or acknowledged unless the sector has track control for the flight or it has been otherwise coordinated.

content.situation: An IAFDOF alert is displayed, an EDCT has been issued to the pilot, and a RAN warning appears for a route processing error on an aircraft your sector has track control for. A controller wants to acknowledge all three codings immediately to clean up the display before taking any action.
content.question_text: When may these codings be acknowledged?

choices.text: All three may be acknowledged immediately to reduce display clutter. The actions can be completed afterward without violating any requirement.
Each coding must be acknowledged only after the appropriate action has been completed. IAFDOF, UTM, and EDCT coding removal before action is completed violates 13-1-9(e).
EDCT may be acknowledged immediately because it was already issued, but IAFDOF and RAN must wait for action completion.
IAFDOF and RAN may be acknowledged immediately, but EDCT requires the pilot to verbally confirm receipt before removal.

content.explanation: IAFDOF, UTM, or EDCT coding must be acknowledged only after the appropriate action has been completed. Acknowledging or removing coding before completing the required action—even to reduce display clutter—violates this requirement. Each coding type has a specific required action that must precede acknowledgment.

content.situation: A red conflict appears in ATOP. A controller wants to override conflict probe using the range/bearing tool because the controller believes the aircraft will actually pass with more separation than probe predicts. The conflict involves a separation standard that conflict probe supports. No facility directive authorizes an override in this circumstance, and control has not been delegated.
content.question_text: What governs whether the controller may override conflict probe using an additional decision support tool?

choices.text: The range/bearing tool always takes precedence over conflict probe when the controller believes probe is inaccurate.
A red conflict requires immediate action, so the range/bearing tool qualifies as an 'other situation' for override purposes.
The controller may override after running a trial probe to confirm the range/bearing calculation.

Additional decision support tools must never be used to override probe results when the applicable separation standard is supported by probe and none of the other override conditions apply. The controller must not override in this situation.

content.explanation: Under no circumstances must the controller utilize any of the additional decision support tools to override probe results when the applicable separation standard is supported by probe and none of the other override conditions apply. The four authorized override conditions are: unrecognized separation standard, conflict resolved with separation ensured, control delegated, or facility directive authorization. Personal belief about probe accuracy is not a valid override reason.

content.situation: An aircraft in ATOP oceanic airspace notifies ATC that its navigation capability has degraded and this affects the applicable separation minima. A controller updates the flight profile but does not select any special indicator on the electronic flight strip for this condition, saying the data block alone is sufficient.
content.question_text: What strip management action is required?

choices.text: Select the Degraded RNP indicator on the electronic flight strip when the aircraft notifies ATC of a reduction in navigation capability that affects applicable separation minima.
No additional strip action is required. The flight profile update is sufficient documentation of the degraded navigation capability.
The 'W' RVSM flag should be selected to indicate the aircraft can no longer maintain RVSM performance.
The aircraft should be removed from the electronic flight strip system and handled with paper strips only.

content.explanation: 13-2-3(C)(1)(c) explicitly requires selecting the Degraded RNP indicator when an aircraft notifies ATC of reduced navigation capability affecting separation minima. This is a specific strip management requirement distinct from profile updates.

content.situation: A CPDLC failure occurs and you are making initial voice contact with the affected aircraft to deliver a revised altitude clearance. The clearance is 'climb and maintain FL370.'
content.question_text: What is the correct phraseology for the initial voice contact with this aircraft?

choices.text: N12345, CPDLC Interrupted, climb and maintain FL370.
N12345, CPDLC Failure, climb and maintain FL370.
N12345, CPDLC has been terminated, climb and maintain FL370.
N12345, reverting to voice communications, climb and maintain FL370.

content.explanation: The exact prescribed phraseology for initial voice contact after a CPDLC failure is '(Call Sign) CPDLC Failure, (message).' This phraseology is mandatory, not discretionary. The controller must say 'CPDLC Failure'—not 'CPDLC Interrupted,' 'CPDLC has been terminated,' or 'reverting to voice communications.' The prescribed wording ensures the flight crew immediately understands the data link status.

content.situation: RNP distance-based longitudinal separation is being applied between two aircraft. A pilot requests a weather deviation. The deviation monitor window shows a pink advisory with a +3 value in the +/- column. A controller says the +3 means 3 extra miles of spacing exist, so the deviation can be approved without further evaluation.
content.question_text: How should the pink advisory with a positive value be interpreted?

choices.text: A positive value means the required spacing has been exceeded, so the deviation may be approved without any additional evaluation.

The pink advisory requires further evaluation before approving the deviation. A zero or positive value in the +/- column indicates the aircraft pair is at or close to required spacing, even with the CDI buffer applied.
A pink advisory is only cautionary and may be disregarded if the controller believes separation appears adequate on the ASD.
The CDI buffer automatically resolves all spacing concerns, so the pilot should be cleared to deviate immediately regardless of the advisory value.

content.explanation: 13-2-5(A) requires further evaluation of pink advisories before approving a weather deviation clearance. 13-2-5(b) explains that zero or positive +/- values mean the aircraft pair is at or close to required spacing — not safely beyond it.

content.situation: At a multiple-controller ATOP workstation, the team begins dividing tasks. One controller wants to handle lower-priority administrative messages before resolving a pending red conflict coordination. Another says facility directives for multiple-person operation are just guidelines once the team has worked together before.
content.question_text: How should task prioritization and team operation be managed?

choices.text: As long as all tasks eventually get done, the order doesn't matter. Facility directives are optional for experienced teams.
Administrative messages should be handled first to clear the queue before addressing any operational tasks.

Complete all ATC tasks according to their priority of duties, which means red conflicts and higher-priority duties take precedence. Accomplish the multiple-controller operation according to facility directives.
Only the designated radar controller needs to follow duty priorities. Other team members can work in any sequence they choose.

content.explanation: 13-2-7(A) requires ensuring all ATC tasks are completed according to their priority of duties — this applies to the whole team. 13-2-7(b) requires the multiple-controller operation to be accomplished according to facility directives. Neither provision is optional.

content.question_text: Which pre-transmission review standard applies to both PDC and CPDLC DCL under Chapter 14?

choices.text: Both PDC and CPDLC DCL require the same review: accuracy, route integrity, completeness, understandability, and route continuity.
PDC requires review for accuracy and route integrity only; CPDLC DCL adds a completeness and continuity check that PDC does not require.
CPDLC DCL requires review for accuracy only; PDC requires the full set of quality checks because it cannot carry amendments.
Neither requires controller review; both are automated systems that self-validate before transmission.

content.explanation: 14-1-1(b) and (c), together with 14-1-2(b), impose identical review obligations for both systems: the controller must review for accuracy and route integrity, and must take action to ensure all information is complete, understandable, and the route is continuous.

content.situation: You are preparing to transmit a PDC for a departure. The clearance looks complete at a glance, but you notice the route segment after the first fix does not connect to the filed destination and a segment in the middle references a waypoint that would be unreadable in the abbreviated format the system displays. A trainee suggests sending it anyway because the pilot can query any issues after receiving the clearance.
content.question_text: How should you handle this PDC before transmission?

choices.text: Send it immediately because PDC clearances are system-generated and do not require controller review.
Transmit the clearance and instruct the trainee to monitor for a pilot query about the route.
Review the clearance for accuracy and route integrity, ensure all information is complete and understandable, and confirm the route is continuous before transmitting.
Send the PDC but add a free-text note in the clearance advising the crew to verify the route with clearance delivery.

content.explanation: 14-1-1(B) and (c) together require review for accuracy and route integrity, and action to ensure completeness, understandability, and route continuity before PDC transmission.

content.question_text: What is the critical operational difference between PDC and CPDLC DCL regarding amended or revised flight plans?

choices.text: PDC must use voice for any change in the filed route, while CPDLC DCL can handle all amendments automatically.

content.situation: A departure has a revised clearance with an amended route that includes a SID with vertical guidance to a published altitude. The CPDLC DCL system can accept and display the amendment, but the specific route change uses a waypoint that is not in the TAS drop-down menu for any selectable field. One controller suggests transmitting what CPDLC can handle and verbally issuing only the problematic waypoint.
content.question_text: How should the amended clearance be handled?

choices.text: Issue the entire clearance verbally because the amendment cannot be fully delivered using the predefined CPDLC fields.
Transmit what CPDLC can handle and verbally issue only the waypoint that is not in the TAS menu.
Use PDC instead since PDC is better suited for amendments that involve SID waypoints.
Override the TAS menu and type the waypoint into any available field to complete the CPDLC transmission.

content.explanation: 14-1-2(c): when a revised flight plan cannot be delivered using CPDLC, it must be verbally issued. An amendment that cannot be expressed through the predefined TAS fields cannot be properly delivered via CPDLC. Partial delivery is not authorized — if any part cannot be delivered, the entire clearance must be verbal.

content.situation: An aircraft is approaching the boundary where it will exit the continental U.S. And transfer to an ATOP oceanic sector. The aircraft has an active CPDLC connection with your sector. A trainee wants to leave the CPDLC connection active through the transfer because the aircraft has an open trajectory-altering clearance that has not yet been acknowledged.
content.question_text: How should you handle the CPDLC connection and the open clearance before transfer?

choices.text: Leave the CPDLC connection active so the ATOP sector can use it, and transfer the open clearance with the handoff.
Ensure no open trajectory-altering clearances remain (unless coordinated), then terminate the CPDLC connection upon transfer.
Terminate the CPDLC connection from any position without coordination, since the transfer to ATOP requires it.
Keep both the CPDLC connection and the open clearance active; the ATOP sector's system will handle both automatically.

content.explanation: 14-2-1(e) requires no open trajectory-altering clearances before transfer unless otherwise coordinated. 14-2-1(g) requires CPDLC termination upon transfer to a non-U.S. facility or ATOP sector. 14-2-1(h) requires coordination with the eligibility sector before terminating from another position.

content.situation: You need to override an open CPDLC route clearance that contained three elements: direct Memphis, cross 30 west of Nashville at FL310, and maintain 280 knots. A trainee wants to cancel the uplink from the ground system first, then issue only the changed speed element by voice, and acknowledge the abnormal indication immediately to clear the FDB.
content.question_text: What is the correct sequence for overriding this multi-element CPDLC clearance?

choices.text: Cancel the CPDLC uplink first to clear the indication, then issue only the changed element by voice.
Use the DISREGARD CPDLC phraseology by voice, restate the entire clearance, instruct the pilot to send UNABLE, then acknowledge the indication after coordination.
Acknowledge the abnormal indication immediately to clear the FDB, then override the clearance element-by-element using CPDLC.
Issue the new speed by voice without referencing the CPDLC clearance; the pilot will see the uplink cancellation automatically.

content.explanation: 14-2-2(c) requires using voice with the full DISREGARD CPDLC phraseology and restating the entire multi-element clearance. 14-2-2(b) requires acknowledgment of abnormal indications only after coordination. 14-2-2(d) requires canceling the uplink only after the pilot acknowledges the superseding clearance via voice.

A trainee wants to send the revised holding instructions via CPDLC because the holding message elements in TBL 14-2-2 support altitude assignment within holding clearances.
content.question_text: Can the revised holding instructions be issued via CPDLC?

choices.text: Yes, because CPDLC holding messages in TBL 14-2-2 can include altitude assignment.
No, because the vertical navigation portion of the climb via procedure must be canceled before CPDLC can be used for holding instructions.
Yes, because holding with altitude assignment via CPDLC is permitted when the aircraft is already in the hold.
No, because holding instructions can never be issued via CPDLC under any circumstances.

A trainee responds by voice because the sector frequency is quiet, and composes the response in free text rather than selecting from the CPDLC message set because it feels more natural to type.
content.question_text: How should the controller respond to the pilot's CPDLC message?

choices.text: Respond by voice since the frequency is available, which is always preferable to data link.
Respond via CPDLC using the standard message set to the extent possible.
Respond via free-text CPDLC, since outside VHF coverage the message set is not required.
Wait for the pilot to call on voice before responding, since CPDLC should not be used for route requests.

content.explanation: 14-3-1(D): when a pilot communicates via CPDLC, respond via CPDLC. 14-3-1(e): prefer the message set over free text. Outside VHF, CPDLC is the primary means of communication.

content.situation: You are working the tower. The surface visibility observation reports 5 miles of prevailing visibility, but from the tower cab you observe that the visibility at tower level is approximately 3 miles due to a low cloud layer that partially obscures your view.
content.explanation: Per 2-6-3a, when tower-level visibility is less than 4 miles, tower personnel must: (1) use the lower of the two observations (tower or surface) for aircraft operations — so 3 miles would be used, (2) forward the tower observation to the weather observer, and (3) notify the weather observer of the visibility decrease below 4 miles.

choices.text: Use the 5-mile surface observation since it is the official weather reporting station value.
Average the two observations and use 4 miles for operations.
Only forward the observation; surface visibility governs aircraft operations regardless.
Use the 3-mile tower observation, forward it to the weather observer, and notify the observer of the decrease below 4 miles.

content.instruction: You receive a SIGMET for severe turbulence covering an area 200 NM east of your sector boundary. The affected area is well outside your 150 NM advisory radius.
content.question_text: Are you required to broadcast this hazardous weather advisory on your frequencies?
content.explanation: No. Per 2-6-6, advisories are not required if aircraft on your frequency(s) will not be affected. The 150 NM radius defines the 'may impact' zone. Since the SIGMET area is 200 NM away and outside your area of impact, and no aircraft on your frequency will be affected, no advisory is required.

choices.text: Yes, because all SIGMETs must be broadcast regardless of distance from your sector.
Yes, because the 200 NM distance only reduces the urgency, not the obligation.
No, because SIGMETs only apply within the CONUS.
No, because the weather is outside the 150 NM area and no aircraft on frequency will be affected.

content.situation: The barometric pressure at your facility has risen to 31.05 inches. A departing aircraft, Delta 123, is climbing and has a mandatory crossing altitude of 1,200 feet AGL before continuing higher.
content.para_context: For departures under high barometric pressure procedures, advise pilots to set 31.00 before any mandatory or crossing altitude, or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.
content.instruction: What phraseology must you issue to Delta 123?
content.explanation: Per 2-7-3b, departures must be told to set the altimeter to 31.00 prior to reaching any mandatory/crossing altitude or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower. The values to compare are the mandatory crossing altitude and 1,500 feet AGL. Here, 1,200 feet is lower, so the instruction must direct the pilot to set 31.00 prior to reaching 1,200 feet AGL.

choices.text: Issue the standard altimeter setting without special phraseology since the aircraft is below 1,500 feet.
Issue the high barometric pressure phraseology directing the pilot to set 31.00 prior to reaching 1,200 feet AGL.
Issue the high barometric pressure arrival phraseology since all aircraft below FL180 use the same procedure.
No special phraseology is needed; the pilot will see the high pressure on their instruments.

content.situation: The touchdown RVR for Runway 27R is 5,500 feet. The mid-point RVR is 1,800 feet. The rollout RVR is 2,500 feet. Prevailing visibility is 3 miles.
content.para_context: Report touchdown RVR when it is 6,000 feet or less. Report mid-point or rollout RVR when the value is below 2,000 feet and touchdown RVR is greater than the secondary value.
content.instruction: Under 2-8-2, determine which RVR values must be reported and why.
content.explanation: Touchdown RVR must be reported because 5,500 feet is a reportable value (6,000 feet or less per 2-8-2a2). Mid-point RVR must also be reported because it is below 2,000 feet while touchdown RVR is greater than 2,000 feet. Rollout RVR is 2,500 feet, so it does not meet the below-2,000-foot secondary-value trigger. Prevailing visibility is above 1 mile, but that does not suppress otherwise reportable RVR.

choices.text: All three values: touchdown is reportable, mid-point is less than 2,000 feet, and 2-8-2b requires both mid-point and rollout because touchdown RVR is greater than the secondary values.
Only touchdown RVR, since prevailing visibility is above 1 mile.
Only touchdown and mid-point RVR, since rollout is above 2,000 feet.
Only mid-point RVR, since it is the lowest value.

content.situation: An aircraft on final approach to Runway 27 suddenly transmits 'Tower, United 1154, wind shear escape.' The aircraft pitches up and begins a full-power climb straight ahead, crossing the path of a departure climbing out of Runway 27. Another controller tells you to immediately issue a turn to both aircraft to restore separation.
content.para_context: If an aircraft under your control informs you that it is performing a wind shear escape, do not issue control instructions that are contrary to pilot actions. ATC should continue to provide safety alerts regarding terrain or obstacles and traffic advisories for the escape aircraft, as appropriate. Once the responding aircraft has initiated a wind shear escape maneuver, the controller is not responsible for providing approved separation between the escape aircraft and any other aircraft.

choices.text: Hand the escape aircraft off to approach control because tower cannot handle escape maneuvers.
Immediately issue turns to both aircraft to restore separation the moment the escape maneuver begins.
Direct the escape aircraft to level off immediately because it is crossing another aircraft's path.
Do not issue contrary control instructions to the escape aircraft. Provide traffic advisories and safety alerts as appropriate, and continue normal control of other aircraft.

content.explanation: 3-1-8D(1) says do not issue contrary instructions during an escape. 3-1-8d(3) says separation responsibility is suspended during the escape. The controller should provide safety alerts and traffic advisories without interfering with the escape maneuver.

content.situation: You are working local control using light signals because the aircraft has no radio. You see two aircraft converging and a collision hazard exists. You direct the alternating red-and-green warning signal to both. The collision hazard resolves, and both aircraft are now clear. One aircraft now needs a landing clearance. Another controller says you must wait until the aircraft acknowledges the warning signal before issuing any other light signal.
content.para_context: The warning signal is not a prohibitive signal and can be followed by any other light signal, as circumstances permit.

choices.text: Wait for an acknowledgment from the aircraft before issuing the landing clearance.
Issue the steady green landing clearance now because the warning signal is not prohibitive and circumstances now permit.
Switch to radio to issue the landing clearance because light signals cannot be used in sequence.
Issue flashing green first as a transition signal, then steady green.

content.explanation: The note explicitly states the warning signal is not prohibitive and can be followed by another light signal as circumstances permit. Since the collision hazard has resolved, the controller may now issue a landing clearance.

Issue the steady green landing clearance now because the warning signal is not prohibitive and circumstances now permit.
Issue flashing green first as a transition signal, then steady green.

content.explanation: The note explicitly states the warning signal is not prohibitive and can be followed by another light signal as circumstances permit. Since the collision hazard has resolved, the controller may now issue a landing clearance.

content.situation: You are controlling a receiver-only helicopter operating at night. You issue a light signal and need acknowledgment. The helicopter is in forward flight after sunset. Another controller advises you to ask for navigation light flashes because 'all aircraft at night use the same acknowledgment method.'.
content.para_context: Receiver-only helicopter acknowledgments at night: Flash landing light or search light. Fixed-wing acknowledgments at night: Flash navigation or landing lights.

choices.text: Request navigation light flashes because all aircraft use the same nighttime acknowledgment under 3-2-3.
Request the helicopter to flash the landing light or search light, because helicopters have a different nighttime procedure.
Request the helicopter to rock the tip path plane because visual acknowledgment is preferred over lights at night.
Skip acknowledgment entirely because helicopters operating at night do not need to acknowledge light signals.

content.explanation: 3-2-3 Specifies different nighttime acknowledgment procedures for helicopters (landing light or search light) versus fixed-wing aircraft (navigation or landing lights). The helicopter-specific procedure must be used.

content.situation: An updated RwyCC report arrives for Runway 9: the codes have changed from 5-5-5 to 3-3-3. The ATIS is currently broadcasting the old report and the next update cycle is 15 minutes away. Several aircraft are on approach and one is holding for departure. Another controller says to just wait for the ATIS cycle since the codes are still being broadcast from the previous report.
content.para_context: When an update to the RwyCC is provided, you must verbally issue the new codes to all aircraft until the ATIS broadcast can be updated.

choices.text: Wait for the ATIS update cycle — ATIS is sufficient for distributing RwyCC information.
Verbally issue the updated codes to all arriving and departing aircraft until the ATIS is updated.
Issue the update only to arriving aircraft because departures do not need RwyCC.
Issue the update only if a pilot directly asks about runway conditions.

content.explanation: 3-3-1E(1)(b) explicitly requires verbal issuance of RwyCC updates to aircraft until the ATIS broadcast can be updated.

content.situation: A pilot reports 'braking is marginal near the fire station on the north side.' The weather is 1/2 mile visibility and night. A heavy B767 and a light general aviation aircraft are both on approach. Another controller says to relay the report as-is because the pilot used clear language.
content.para_context: Use approved braking-action terms only. If a pilot uses non-approved terms, ask them to recategorize. Describe affected runway portions using runway-relative terms (e.g., first half, last half), not extraneous landmarks like the fire station, especially at night or in low visibility.

choices.text: Ask the pilot to recategorize using approved terms, obtain a runway-relative location description, and include the reporting aircraft type in the advisory.
Issue the report as 'marginal near the fire station' because the pilot's plain language is better than approved terms in low visibility.
Issue the report as 'medium to poor' without asking the pilot, because 'marginal' maps to that approved term.
Suppress the report because it was given in non-approved terms and cannot be used.

content.explanation: The controller must standardize the terminology by asking the pilot to recategorize, and must describe the affected runway area without relying on extraneous landmarks.

content.situation: A military F-16 is on final approach. Another controller on position mentions the aircraft had reported a possible hydraulic issue during taxi but continued. No one has requested the arresting system be raised. The F-16 is a military jet aircraft landing with a condition that may indicate an arresting system is needed.
content.para_context: Raise aircraft arresting systems whenever a military jet aircraft is landing with known or suspected conditions (drag chute/hydraulic/electrical failure, etc.) that indicate an arresting system may be needed. Exceptions apply for aircraft that cannot engage arresting systems.

choices.text: Raise the arresting system and inform the pilot of the barrier/cable position.
Do nothing — only a specific pilot or military authority request can trigger raising the system.
Ask the pilot whether they want the system raised before taking any action.
Raise the system only if the pilot also reports a radio failure.

content.explanation: 3-3-6B(3) independently authorizes raising the system when a military jet lands with suspected hydraulic failure — no pilot or authority request is needed.

content.situation: Weather is below CAT II minimums and the FFM remote status unit alarms while an arriving aircraft is outside the middle marker on the ILS to Runway 27. You can see a maintenance vehicle in the critical area from the tower. Another controller says to immediately tell the pilot the localizer is unreliable.
content.para_context: When the FFM alarms and the aircraft is outside the MM (or 1/2 mile final), check for visible critical-area encroachment. If the alarm has not cleared before the aircraft reaches the MM (or 1/2 mile final), immediately issue the advisory that the localizer is unreliable.

choices.text: Immediately issue the advisory — the alarm always triggers an advisory regardless of aircraft position.
Direct the vehicle out of the critical area. If the alarm persists until the aircraft reaches the MM (or 1/2 mile final), issue the unreliable-localizer advisory.
Cancel the approach because critical-area encroachment during CAT II weather is a mandatory missed-approach trigger.
Do nothing — the alarm is clearly caused by the vehicle and the localizer is actually fine.

content.explanation: The outside-MM procedure includes checking for and resolving visible encroachments first. The advisory is only issued if the alarm has not cleared by the MM or 1/2 mile final.

content.instruction: Determine the correct response for this terminal operation.
content.situation: You are working terminal radar. Primary radar fails suddenly. Secondary radar remains operational. The failure is expected to be temporary, and the advisory is already on the ATIS recording. A pilot checks in and states they have ATIS information.
content.question_text: What must you do with respect to the primary radar unavailability advisory for this pilot?

choices.text: Issue the full primary-radar-unavailable advisory because ATIS does not substitute for individual pilot notification.
Omit the advisory because it is on ATIS and the pilot indicated having the ATIS information.
Issue only 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' and switch to nonradar procedures.
Ask the pilot whether they want the advisory before deciding.

content.explanation: Under 5-1-2 note, the primary-radar-unavailable advisory may be omitted when provided on ATIS and the pilot indicates having ATIS information.

content.instruction: Determine the correct response for this terminal operation.
content.situation: Terminal radar is being severely jammed by EA activity and you need the activity stopped immediately. Through the normal ARTCC path will take too long. An EA aircraft is visible on the scope and within emergency frequency range.
content.question_text: What is the correct procedure?

choices.text: Wait for the ARTCC regardless of urgency because terminal facilities may never contact EA aircraft directly.
Broadcast the stop request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC of the direct broadcast as soon as possible.
Continue providing degraded radar service until the ARTCC acts on its own.
Request suspension through the destination tower instead of the ARTCC.

content.explanation: 5-1-3B2 permits terminal facilities to broadcast directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency when immediate cessation is required, with ARTCC notification as soon as possible afterward.

content.instruction: Determine whether merging target procedures apply.
content.situation: A radar-identified piston aircraft is cruising at 9,500 feet and is not in a holding pattern. Its target appears likely to merge with another covered aircraft's target.
content.question_text: Does this piston aircraft receive merging target procedures?

choices.text: Yes, because it is radar-identified and below 10,000 feet.
Yes, because any radar-identified aircraft receives merging target procedures.
No, merging target procedures only apply above FL180.
No, because it is below 10,000 feet, is not a turbojet, and is not a presidential aircraft.

content.explanation: Merging target procedures apply to three categories: aircraft at or above 10,000 feet, all turbojets regardless of altitude, and presidential aircraft. A piston aircraft at 9,500 feet falls into none of these categories.

content.instruction: Determine whether a separate termination advisory is required.
content.situation: An aircraft cancels its IFR flight plan while inside Class B airspace.
content.question_text: Must the controller issue a separate 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' advisory?

choices.text: No, because IFR cancellation always triggers automatic termination without advisory.
No, because the aircraft is in controlled airspace and radar service cannot be terminated at all.
Yes, because IFR cancellation inside Class B airspace is excepted from the automatic termination rule.
Yes, but only if the pilot specifically requests it.

content.explanation: IFR cancellation is listed as an automatic termination situation, but the paragraph explicitly excepts Class B, Class C, TRSA, and areas where basic radar service is provided. Inside Class B, the controller must still issue the termination advisory.

content.instruction: What action must you take and what phraseology do you use?
content.situation: During a surveillance approach, you observe radical target deviations that make a safe approach questionable. The aircraft has not yet passed decision height.
content.question_text: What should you do?

choices.text: Allow the approach to continue and monitor
Instruct: IF RUNWAY/APPROACH LIGHTS NOT IN SIGHT, EXECUTE MISSED APPROACH, or climb to/maintain specified altitude and fly specified course
Issue a go-around only if the pilot requests it
Immediately terminate radar service

content.explanation: 5-10-14A requires instructing the aircraft if runway environment not in sight to execute missed approach or climb/maintain altitude and fly specified course.

content.instruction: What do you need to ensure?

Only notify the watch supervisor
Have all aircraft change to another frequency

content.explanation: 5-14-4A requires informing all appropriate positions before terminating and transferring or terminating all pertinent flight data.

content.instruction: In what order should tracks be suspended?
content.situation: Data block overlap in a holding pattern has created an unworkable situation on your STARS display. Some tracks display automatic altitude readouts and some do not.
content.question_text: Which tracks should be suspended first?

choices.text: Those not displaying automatic altitude readouts first; then those with altitude readouts if needed
Those displaying automatic altitude readouts first
All tracks simultaneously
The newest tracks first

content.explanation: 5-14-8: Suspend tracks without altitude readouts first. Only if condition still exists, suspend those with altitude readouts.

content.situation: A VFR aircraft with an inoperative transponder at 12,000 feet MSL requests a deviation from Mode C requirements. The pilot refuses to file IFR and will not provide a VFR route of flight.
content.question_text: May you approve this deviation request?

choices.text: Yes, all VFR deviation requests must be approved.
Yes, if the pilot states it is an emergency.
No, 5-2-19d states a deviation shall not be approved unless the aircraft has filed IFR or provided a VFR route with radio contact maintained.
Yes, as long as the aircraft is outside Class B airspace.

content.explanation: 5-2-19D prohibits approving an inflight deviation unless the aircraft has filed IFR or a VFR route of flight is provided and radio contact with ATC is maintained. The emergency exception (5-2-19a) applies to aircraft without transponder equipment installed, not inoperative equipment.
content.para_context: INFLIGHT DEVIATIONS FROM TRANSPONDER/MODE C REQUIREMENTS

content.instruction: Determine the correct response to ADS-B malfunction.
content.situation: A civil aircraft's ADS-B transmitter appears to be malfunctioning and the malfunction is jeopardizing the safe execution of air traffic control functions.
content.question_text: What must the controller do?

choices.text: Only notify the OS/CIC and continue normal operations.
Instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC.
Terminate radar service for that aircraft immediately.
Instruct the aircraft to squawk standby and continue on primary radar only.

content.explanation: 5-2-22B: when a malfunctioning ADS-B transmitter is jeopardizing the safe execution of ATC functions, the controller must instruct the aircraft to stop ADS-B transmissions and notify the OS/CIC. The phraseology is 'STOP ADS-B TRANSMISSIONS, AND IF ABLE, SQUAWK THREE/ALFA (code).'

No, automated association is only valid in terminal facilities.
Yes, but only if the controller manually verifies the beacon code by requesting a code change.
Yes, because the full data block is automatically associated with the target symbol of an aircraft squawking a computer-assigned discrete code.

content.explanation: 5-3-3D states that en route, an aircraft may be considered identified when the full data block is automatically associated with the target symbol of an aircraft squawking a discrete code assigned by the computer.

content.situation: Two aircraft targets are in close proximity on your scope. You observe an identifying turn of approximately 35 degrees, but both targets appear to make a similar turn at nearly the same moment. You now doubt which target belongs to your aircraft.
content.question_text: What is the correct response?

choices.text: Continue tracking both targets until one diverges; radar identification is not affected by proximity.
Assume the target closest to the filed route is the correct one.
Use more than one method of identification immediately. If doubt remains, take immediate action to re-identify or terminate radar service.
Terminate radar service on both targets and request they re-establish contact.

content.explanation: 5-3-5A requires using more than one method when proximity or duplication cause doubt. 5-3-5b requires immediate re-identification or termination if identification remains questionable.

content.situation: During prearranged coordination, you will penetrate another controller's airspace. You have three aircraft from that controller's sector that will be in the penetration area. One of them is a VFR aircraft that has already had radar services terminated and is clear of Class B and Class C airspace, but the delegated airspace extends beyond Class C.
content.question_text: What data block display is required for each aircraft?

choices.text: All three must display at least position symbol and altitude information regardless of radar service status.
All three must display flight identification and altitude information at minimum.
No data block display is required for any aircraft during prearranged coordination.
For the other two aircraft: position symbol and altitude information at minimum. The VFR aircraft clear of Class B/C with radar services terminated needs no data block retention.

content.explanation: 5-3-9B requires position symbol and altitude information for prearranged coordination penetration. The note exempts VFR aircraft clear of Class B/C with radar services terminated where delegated airspace extends beyond those classes.

content.situation: You need to transfer radar identification of an aircraft to another controller. The aircraft will enter the receiving controller's airspace, and you WILL transfer radio communications with the aircraft.
content.question_text: What coordination action applies?

choices.text: Point out.
Handoff.
Traffic.
Prearranged coordination.

content.explanation: 5-4-2A defines a handoff as the action for transferring radar identification when the aircraft enters the receiving controller's airspace AND communications will be transferred. A point out (5-4-2c) is for when communications will NOT be transferred.

content.situation: You have accepted a handoff from a transferring controller for an aircraft approaching your sector. After acceptance, you realize you must delay the aircraft's descent through the transferring controller's vertical limits. You have kept communications with the transferring controller open.
content.question_text: What must you do?

choices.text: Advise the transferring controller as soon as possible that you will delay the descent through their vertical limits.
Issue the delay to the pilot without informing the transferring controller.
Cancel the handoff and re-initiate it later.
There is no requirement to advise the transferring controller of a descent delay.

content.explanation: 5-4-6H requires the receiving controller to advise the transferring controller as soon as possible if they will delay the aircraft's climb or descent through the transferring controller's vertical limits.

content.situation: An aircraft was issued a 'descend via' clearance on the Ksino One arrival. You now vector the aircraft off the STAR for traffic. You later instruct the aircraft to resume the procedure.
content.question_text: What must you include when clearing the aircraft to resume the procedure?

choices.text: Simply say 'resume the Ksino One arrival' because the restrictions reactivate automatically.
Only issue the next waypoint crossing restriction and maintain the current altitude.
Issue or reissue all applicable published crossing restrictions, or instruct the aircraft to Descend Via the STAR.
Cancel the STAR entirely and issue a completely new clearance.

content.explanation: 5-6-2F requires issuing/reissuing all applicable restrictions or instructing Climb Via/Descend Via when resuming a procedure with published crossing restrictions.

content.question_text: May you vector the aircraft using the DVA while it is on a radar SID?

Yes, but only after the aircraft reaches the MVA.
Yes, if the pilot requests radar vectors.

content.situation: An arriving aircraft is 3 miles from the approach gate. The reported ceiling is 600 feet above the MVA and visibility is 5 miles.
content.question_text: Can you vector this aircraft to intercept the final approach course closer than 2 miles outside the approach gate?

choices.text: Yes, because ceiling is at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles. Vector no closer than the approach gate.
No, the minimum is always 2 miles outside the approach gate.
Yes, vector to intercept at the final approach fix.
Only if the pilot specifically requests it.

content.explanation: 5-9-1A1 permits vectors closer than 2 miles when ceiling is at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA and visibility is at least 3 miles.

content.instruction: What must you tell the pilot and what phraseology do you use?
content.situation: You need to vector an arriving aircraft across the final approach course for spacing.
content.question_text: What is the correct action?

choices.text: Just issue the vector without informing the pilot.
Issue the vector and tell the pilot to turn inbound at their discretion.
Inform the aircraft and state the reason. Say: EXPECT VECTORS ACROSS FINAL FOR SPACING.
Issue a missed approach instead.

content.explanation: 5-9-3 Requires informing the aircraft whenever a vector will take it across the final approach course and stating the reason.

content.question_text: What is the reference point for calculating the 5-minute action deadline?

choices.text: The time the last position report was actually received.
The time the flight plan was filed.
The time the aircraft was estimated over the fix.
The time the controller first notices the missing report.

content.situation: Aircraft N123 was estimated to cross BOWIE intersection at 1415Z. At 1418Z you realize no position report has been received. N123 is at the same altitude as N456, which is 15 miles behind on the same airway and also expects to cross BOWIE.
content.question_text: What is the correct action?

choices.text: Wait until 1420Z exactly because the section requires waiting the full 5 minutes.
Take action to obtain the position report immediately; the report affects separation and 5 minutes from the estimate is 1420Z.
Do nothing until N456 also crosses BOWIE because separation is not yet lost.
Issue a new clearance to N456 for a different altitude and disregard the missing report.

content.explanation: 6-1-2 Requires taking action no later than 5 minutes after the estimate over the fix. Since the position report affects separation (N456 is trailing at the same altitude), action must be taken by 1420Z. The controller should not wait until the deadline to begin acting.

content.question_text: An aircraft is crossing a fix that serves as a reporting point for both an en route center and an approach control. The center has already received the position report. Under may the approach control also require the pilot to report that fix?

choices.text: Yes, because the restriction applies only when both facilities are the same type.
No; the same position report must not be required by more than one facility.
Yes, because the approach control did not hear the report made to the center.
Yes, provided the report was made more than 5 minutes ago to the first facility.

content.explanation: The applicable rule is an absolute prohibition: do not require an aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility. Facility type is irrelevant.

content.situation: An aircraft on a cruise clearance at 8,000 feet has verbally reported leaving 8,000 in descent and reported passing through 7,000 feet (1,000 feet below, the appropriate minimum for IFR below FL290). Another aircraft requests 8,000 feet.
content.question_text: May you assign 8,000 feet to the second aircraft?

choices.text: No; a cruise clearance prevents altitude reassignment until the cruising altitude restriction is canceled.
Yes, but only if you use Mode C to confirm the aircraft is below 8,000.
No; the cruise aircraft must report reaching the MIA before 8,000 can be reassigned.
Yes; the cruise aircraft reported leaving 8,000 (cannot return to it) and reported passing through 7,000 — satisfying the stricter 6-6-2c2 requirement.

content.explanation: Per the applicable rule, the stricter rule applies: the aircraft must report at or passing through another altitude separated from the original by the appropriate minimum. The pilot reported leaving 8,000 (cannot return to it per the note) and passed through 7,000 (1,000 ft below = appropriate minimum). However, do NOT use Mode C to effect this separation per the explicit prohibition in c2.

content.situation: An IFR aircraft at FL330 in Class A airspace requests a VFR climb to FL350 so the pilot can avoid an area of reported moderate turbulence that another pilot described visually. The pilot states they can maintain their own separation in VMC.
content.para_context: Per 7-1-1, do not apply visual separation or issue VFR or VFR-on-top clearances in Class A airspace.
content.question_text: How should the controller respond to this request?

choices.text: Approve the request if the pilot accepts responsibility for separation.
Deny the VFR climb request and instead issue an IFR altitude change to FL350 or an appropriate alternative IFR clearance.
Issue a VFR-on-top clearance since the aircraft is already on an IFR flight plan and can remain VMC.
Apply visual separation and approve the climb while the pilot maintains visual contact.

content.explanation: Per 7-1-1, VFR clearances and VFR-on-top clearances are prohibited in Class A airspace, and visual separation may not be applied. The controller must handle this request using IFR procedures only, such as an IFR altitude change.

content.instruction: The controller cleared the aircraft to descend VFR within a defined altitude block. Which pilot readback is correct?

choices.text: Maintain VFR conditions below 5,000.
Descend VFR, maintain 4,000 until further advised.
Descend VFR, between 5,000 and 3,000.
Descend VFR, climbing between 3,000 and 5,000.

content.explanation: Per 7-1-2 phraseology, CLIMB/DESCEND VFR can be followed by 'BETWEEN (altitude) AND (altitude)' to specify an altitude block. MAINTAIN VFR CONDITIONS with ABOVE/BELOW is a different phraseology variant.

content.question_text: A terminal controller clears an IFR departure to maintain VFR conditions for noise-abatement reasons. A trainee questions the clearance because the IFR departure route published in the facility SOP already conforms to the FAA-approved noise-abatement route. Is the clearance valid, and why?

choices.text: Yes, because any noise-abatement concern justifies the clearance regardless of whether the route conforms.
Yes, because the terminal noise-abatement condition applies regardless of route conformity.
No, because VFR-conditions clearances for noise abatement may only be issued by en route facilities.

No, because the noise-abatement condition requires that the IFR departure route does NOT conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude. If the route already conforms, the condition is not met.

content.explanation: Per 7-1-2a2, the noise-abatement condition requires that 'part of the IFR departure route does not conform to an FAA-approved noise-abatement route or altitude.' If the route already conforms, this condition is not satisfied and the clearance cannot be issued under this provision.

content.question_text: What does a pilot's use of 'have numbers' actually mean for the controller?

choices.text: The pilot has received the ATIS broadcast and the controller may omit all landing information.
The controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter if the procedures allow it, but it does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast.
The pilot is reporting the current ATIS code to the controller.
The pilot has all known traffic information and the controller must not issue additional advisories.

content.explanation: Per 7-1-3 and its note, 'have numbers' may support omitting wind, runway, and altimeter, but it does not indicate that the pilot received the ATIS broadcast. The ATIS code and 'have numbers' are separate concepts.

content.question_text: What two actions must be taken when it becomes necessary to hold VFR aircraft at visual holding fixes?

choices.text: Clear to prominent geographical fixes recognizable from the air, preferably on sectional charts; and issue traffic information to aircraft at the same fix.
Issue an IFR clearance to each VFR aircraft and assign a discrete squawk code.
Coordinate with all adjacent sectors and issue approach clearances.
Assign holding altitudes and speed restrictions to every VFR aircraft in the hold, regardless of traffic at the same fix.

content.explanation: Per 7-1-4a and 7-1-4b, the two required actions are: clear aircraft to hold at prominent, recognizable geographical fixes (preferably on sectional charts), and issue traffic information to aircraft cleared to hold at the same fix. IFR clearances and squawk codes are not required for VFR visual holding.

content.question_text: What should the controller use as the visual holding fix for this unfamiliar pilot?

choices.text: Use the lake since it is prominent and recognizable, even though it is not a charted VFR checkpoint.
Select a depicted VFR checkpoint because the pilot does not exhibit local familiarity.
Ask the pilot to pick any geographic feature and hold over it.
Assign the airport as the holding fix.

content.explanation: Per 7-1-4 note, depicted VFR checkpoints are preferred unless the pilot shows local familiarity. Since this pilot is unfamiliar, a depicted VFR checkpoint should be selected.

content.situation: An en route controller has a pilot maintaining visual separation from another aircraft. The radar targets begin to converge on the display. The controller has already issued the instruction to maintain visual separation but did not issue a merging advisory at that time.
content.para_context: Per 7-2-1b(f) and the associated note: 'Advise the pilots if the radar targets appear likely to merge.' The note states to issue this advisory in conjunction with the instruction to maintain visual separation, the advisory to the other aircraft of the converging course, or thereafter if the controller subsequently becomes aware that targets are merging.
content.question_text: What should the controller do about the merging targets becoming apparent after the initial instruction?

choices.text: The controller must issue the merging advisory because the note allows issuing it 'thereafter if the controller subsequently becomes aware that the targets are merging.'
The controller cannot issue a merging advisory now because it must be given only at the time of the initial visual separation instruction.
The controller should cancel visual separation and issue vectors, because merging targets indicate the procedure is unsafe.
No additional advisory is needed because the pilot already accepted visual separation responsibility.

content.explanation: Per the note to 7-2-1b(f), the merging advisory can be issued 'thereafter if the controller subsequently becomes aware that the targets are merging.' This allows the controller to issue it later if the merging condition was not apparent initially.

content.situation: It is one hour after sunset and a controller has two IFR aircraft holding at the same fix. One aircraft requests VFR-on-top. The controller considers clearing both aircraft VFR-on-top to separate them without IFR vertical separation.
content.para_context: Per 7-3-1c, do not clear aircraft to maintain VFR-on-top between sunset and sunrise to separate holding aircraft from each other or from en route aircraft unless restrictions are applied to ensure the appropriate IFR vertical separation.
content.question_text: Can the controller use VFR-on-top to separate the holding aircraft at night?

choices.text: Yes, but only with altitude restrictions applied to ensure appropriate IFR vertical separation.
No, VFR-on-top is never permitted for separation of any aircraft after sunset.
Yes, VFR-on-top may be used freely at night because the pilot's see-and-avoid responsibility is sufficient.
No, because VFR-on-top is not authorized in holding patterns regardless of time of day.

content.explanation: Per 7-3-1c, VFR-on-top can be used to separate holding aircraft at night, but ONLY if altitude restrictions are applied to ensure IFR vertical separation. The phraseology 'MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP AT OR ABOVE/BELOW/BETWEEN (altitudes)' provides this restriction.

content.question_text: What VFR cruising altitude should a VFR-on-top aircraft use when flying a magnetic course of 090 degrees at 5,500 feet MSL?

choices.text: Odd cardinal altitude plus 500 feet — in this case, 5,500 feet is correct.
Even cardinal altitude plus 500 feet — the aircraft should be at 4,500 or 6,500 feet.
Any altitude above 3,000 feet AGL, because VFR-on-top aircraft are not bound by cruising altitude rules.
The altitude assigned by ATC in the IFR clearance, not a VFR cruising altitude.

content.explanation: Per 7-3-2 and 14 CFR 91.159(a), magnetic courses 0-179 use odd cardinal altitudes plus 500 feet. At 5,500 feet with a course of 090, the aircraft is at the correct altitude.

content.situation: A VFR-on-top aircraft reports at 5,500 feet MSL on a magnetic course of 270 degrees, operating above 3,000 feet AGL. A radar report indicates the aircraft's altitude does not match the required VFR cruising altitude for its direction of flight.
content.para_context: Per 7-3-2, when a report indicates the pilot is not complying with 14 CFR 91.159(a), inform the aircraft of the proper VFR-on-top cruising levels. For magnetic courses 180-359, the proper altitudes are even altitudes plus 500 feet.
content.question_text: What should the controller tell this pilot?

choices.text: VFR-ON-TOP CRUISING LEVELS FOR YOUR DIRECTION OF FLIGHT ARE EVEN ALTITUDES PLUS FIVE HUNDRED FEET.
5,500 feet is correct; no advisory is needed.
Resume your own navigation at an IFR altitude because VFR-on-top cruising rules do not apply below FL 180.
VFR-ON-TOP CRUISING LEVELS FOR YOUR DIRECTION OF FLIGHT ARE ODD ALTITUDES PLUS FIVE HUNDRED FEET.

content.explanation: Per 7-3-2 and 14 CFR 91.159(a), westbound courses (180-359) use even altitudes plus 500 feet. At 5,500 feet (odd + 500), the aircraft is at the wrong altitude for its direction of flight and should be at an even altitude plus 500 feet like 4,500 or 6,500.

content.situation: An aircraft on a visual approach at a towered airport executes a go-around. The controller needs to get the aircraft back into the IFR system quickly. The airport has a standard traffic pattern altitude of 1,000 feet AGL but the MVA is 2,500 feet MSL.
content.para_context: Per 7-4-1a, the controller may direct the go-around to: (1) enter the traffic pattern — no altitude assignment required, pilot climbs to pattern altitude and is responsible for terrain/obstruction, OR (2) proceed as otherwise instructed — ATC is responsible to provide instructions to facilitate a climb to the minimum IFR altitude.
content.question_text: Which go-around option should the controller select to get the aircraft to the MVA most reliably?

choices.text: Use option a2 to issue specific instructions facilitating a climb to the minimum IFR altitude, because the MVA exceeds pattern altitude and the controller is responsible for ensuring the aircraft reaches it.
Use option a1 directing the aircraft to enter the traffic pattern, because all go-arounds at towered airports must use the pattern.
Issue the published missed approach for the instrument procedure, because go-arounds from visual approaches must follow instrument missed approach segments.
Cancel the IFR flight plan because visual approaches have no missed approach segment, so the aircraft must proceed VFR.

content.explanation: Per 7-4-1a2, under option a2 ATC is responsible to provide instructions to facilitate a climb to the minimum altitude for instrument operations. Since the MVA exceeds pattern altitude, option a2 is more appropriate. Under a1, the pilot would climb to pattern altitude only (lower than MVA), which may not provide terrain/obstruction clearance for IFR operations.

content.situation: A controller is clearing an aircraft for the 'RIVER VISUAL RUNWAY 27' CVFP. The reported ceiling is 600 feet above the airport, the MVA is 1,500 feet MSL, and the airport elevation is 500 feet MSL. The aircraft is not following another aircraft on the approach.
content.para_context: Per 7-4-5d, for an aircraft not following another on the approach, the reported ceiling must be at least 500 feet above the MVA/MIA.
content.question_text: Can the controller issue the CVFP clearance?

choices.text: No, because for an aircraft not following another, the reported ceiling (600 ft above airport = 1,100 ft MSL) is not at least 500 feet above the MVA (1,500 ft MSL).
Yes, because the ceiling is at least 500 feet above the airport elevation, which is all that is required.
Yes, because the ceiling only needs to be above the MVA by any amount; the 500-foot margin applies only to following aircraft.
No, because CVFPs may only be issued when the ceiling is at least 1,000 feet above the MVA.

content.explanation: Per 7-4-5d, the 500-foot threshold is measured above the MVA/MIA, not above the airport elevation. With a ceiling of 1,100 ft MSL (600 ft AGL + 500 ft airport) and an MVA of 1,500 ft MSL, the ceiling is only 400 ft below the MVA — not 500 ft above it. The clearance cannot be issued.

With a ceiling of 1,100 ft MSL (600 ft AGL + 500 ft airport) and an MVA of 1,500 ft MSL, the ceiling is only 400 ft below the MVA — not 500 ft above it. The clearance cannot be issued.

content.situation: An SVFR fixed-wing aircraft needs vertical separation from an IFR aircraft at 3,000 feet MSL. The MSA for the area is 1,500 feet MSL. The ceiling is 1,200 feet overcast.
content.para_context: Per 7-5-4, do not assign a fixed altitude. Clear the SVFR aircraft at or below an altitude which is at least 500 feet below conflicting IFR traffic but not below the MSA. The pilot must also remain clear of clouds.

MAINTAIN 2,500 (a fixed altitude to ensure vertical separation).

CLIMB AND MAINTAIN 3,500 (above the IFR traffic).

content.situation: Ground visibility is officially reported at 3/4 mile at a towered airport. A fixed-wing aircraft requests SVFR departure. The aircraft is not a scheduled air carrier.
content.para_context: Per 7-5-7, when ground visibility is below 1 mile, advise the pilot of the visibility and ask intentions. Scheduled air carriers may not be authorized SVFR unless the pilot indicates an emergency.
content.question_text: How should the controller handle this fixed-wing SVFR departure request?

choices.text: Deny the request because ground visibility is below 1 mile.
Advise the pilot of the visibility and ask the pilot's intentions.
Authorize SVFR immediately because the visibility is below 1 mile and the pilot has already requested it.
Issue a special VFR clearance only if the aircraft has filed a flight plan.

content.explanation: Per 7-5-7, when ground visibility is below 1 mile for fixed-wing operations, tell the pilot the visibility and ask intentions. The pilot can then decide whether to proceed knowing the visibility is below the normal SVFR minimum.

content.situation: A pilot requests a VFR flight plan from Miami to San Juan at FL190 within the Miami Oceanic CTA. Weather is clear and it is midday.
content.para_context: Under 8-1-3, VFR flight-plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs are permitted only below FL 180, between sunrise and sunset, and in weather at or above 14 CFR 91.155.

choices.text: Approve the flight plan because the weather and time conditions are met.
Deny the VFR flight plan at FL190 because VFR operations in the Miami Oceanic CTA are limited to below FL180.
Approve the flight plan if the pilot files IFR instead but operates VFR in practice.
Approve the flight plan because FL190 is within the all-Oceanic-FIR exception below the CTAs.

content.question_text: A controller uses lateral separation between two aircraft in oceanic airspace. Under 8-1-4, is this acceptable?

choices.text: No, because lateral separation is not listed in 8-1-4 among the approved types.

No, because lateral separation may only be used in offshore airspace, not oceanic airspace.

content.situation: You are working an oceanic sector with no radar coverage. Two aircraft at the same flight level are on crossing tracks. You need to establish separation.
content.para_context: Under 8-1-4, separation must consist of at least one of the following: vertical separation; horizontal separation by longitudinal or lateral means; or radar separation where radar coverage is adequate.

choices.text: Rely on radar separation only because crossing-track aircraft always need Chapter 5 radar rules.
Use vertical separation by assigning one aircraft a different flight level, or use horizontal separation by longitudinal or lateral means.
Tell the pilots to maintain visual separation because oceanic sectors always have visual conditions.
Apply no separation because crossing tracks are automatically separated by time.

content.explanation: Per 8-1-4, without radar coverage, the controller must use vertical or horizontal (longitudinal or lateral) separation.

content.question_text: A controller is working an oceanic control area and an en route chart depicts a different altimeter setting procedure for a portion of the sector. What should the controller do in that charted area?

choices.text: Apply the charted procedure only within 50 NM of the shoreline.
Ignore the chart and use flight levels with 29.92 because 8-1-5 is the controlling authority.
Use whichever procedure the pilot prefers for the area in question.
Follow the charted procedure because 8-1-5 permits departure from the flight-level/29.92 rule when charted otherwise.

content.explanation: Per 8-1-5, the flight-level/29.92 rule applies unless directed and/or charted otherwise. A charted exception takes precedence.

content.situation: An aircraft is transitioning from domestic airspace into an oceanic control area. The pilot is still using the coastal altimeter setting and is flying at an assigned MSL altitude.
content.para_context: Under 8-1-5, within oceanic control areas, unless directed or charted otherwise, altitude assignment must be based on flight levels and a standard altimeter setting of 29.92 inches Hg.

choices.text: Ignore the altimeter setting because oceanic operations do not require a standard barometric reference.
Allow the aircraft to keep the coastal altimeter setting because transitioning is optional once offshore.
Instruct the pilot to use the destination altimeter setting throughout the oceanic portion.
If no direction or charting provides otherwise, transition the aircraft to flight levels with the standard 29.92 altimeter setting upon entering the oceanic control area.

content.explanation: Per 8-1-5, within oceanic control areas, altitude assignments use flight levels and the standard 29.92 setting unless directed or charted otherwise.

content.situation: An aircraft in oceanic airspace has not sent a position report that affects separation between it and a following aircraft on the same track. The control estimate was 8 minutes ago, and no other timing specification applies.
content.para_context: Under 8-1-6, when a position report affecting separation is not received, take action to obtain the report no later than 10 minutes after the control estimate unless otherwise specified.

choices.text: Take action now to obtain the report before the 10-minute deadline passes.
Wait until 10 minutes have passed exactly before taking any action.
Wait for the next routine contact because 8 minutes is not yet a concern.
Assume the aircraft crossed the fix and update separation based on the estimate alone.

content.explanation: Per 8-1-6, the controller may take action at any point up to the 10-minute deadline. Since 8 minutes have passed, action should be taken before the deadline expires.

content.situation: You observe an aircraft in oceanic airspace that is 500 feet off its assigned flight level in RVSM airspace. A trainee asks if this qualifies as a reportable height error under the oceanic error reporting program.
content.para_context: Under 8-1-7, FAA Order JO 7210.632 establishes procedures for reporting Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations verification in oceanic airspace.

choices.text: Follow the JO 7210.632 reporting procedures because height errors are a listed reportable category.
No report is needed because height errors are only reportable if separation is lost.
Report it as a gross navigation error since the aircraft is off altitude.
Ignore it because height errors are only reportable in domestic airspace.

content.situation: You are establishing time-based separation between two oceanic aircraft. The automation system provides one estimate, but a pilot position report suggests a different position. The automation estimate appears more recent.
content.para_context: Under 8-1-8, control estimates may be determined by the ATC automation system or calculated by the controller using known wind patterns, previous transit times, pilot progress reports, and pilot estimates. Estimates may be updated through automated position reports or manually.

choices.text: Use the automation system estimate but verify it against available data; control estimates may be updated through automated position reports or manually as needed.
Discard the automation estimate and use only the pilot report because pilot data always supersedes automation.
Average the two positions and use the midpoint for all separation calculations.
Ignore both and use only the filed flight plan time for separation.

content.explanation: Per 8-1-8, control estimates may be determined by automation or calculated by the controller using various inputs including pilot reports. The controller may update estimates as better information becomes available.

content.situation: A non-RVSM aircraft is being delivered from the manufacturer to its first operator and requests transit through oceanic RVSM airspace. The operations supervisor has approved the exception. Traffic is light.
content.para_context: Non-RVSM aircraft may not be permitted in RVSM airspace unless they meet excepted aircraft criteria and are previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC. Initial delivery aircraft are listed as excepted. Exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis.

choices.text: Deny the request because delivery aircraft are never excepted in oceanic airspace.
The aircraft may be permitted because it qualifies as excepted (initial delivery), has supervisor approval, and conditions permit.
Permit only if the aircraft files IFR and descends below FL290.
Permit without any approval because delivery flights are automatically exempt.

content.explanation: Per the non-RVSM exception rules: initial delivery aircraft are listed as excepted, require prior supervisor/CIC approval, and are accommodated on a workload/traffic-permitting basis. All three conditions are met here.

content.situation: An oceanic flight's estimate at the boundary has changed by 5 minutes since the last coordination. The receiving facility typically needs 10 minutes to analyze new data before accepting control. The protected airspace will overlap the common boundary.
content.para_context: Under 8-2-1, ARTCCs must coordinate in sufficient time for the receiving facility to analyze data and effect additional coordination (timing may be in an LOA), coordinate when protected airspace overlaps a common boundary, and forward revisions of estimates of 3 minutes or more.

choices.text: Forward the revised estimate (it exceeds the 3-minute threshold), coordinate the boundary overlap, and provide data early enough for the receiving facility to act.
Forward the estimate revision and boundary overlap information simultaneously at the moment the aircraft crosses the boundary.
Only coordinate the boundary overlap because estimate revisions are forwarded only on pilot request.
Send the information after the aircraft checks in with the next facility because oceanic coordination is post-hoc.

content.explanation: Per 8-2-1, a 5-minute change exceeds the 3-minute threshold for forwarding, coordination must allow sufficient time, and boundary overlap requires coordination.

content.situation: You are the accepting controller. Nonradar separation is being applied. The aircraft is estimated to reach your boundary in 3 minutes, communications have not been transferred yet, and the transferring facility has not sent a needed flight plan amendment.
content.para_context: Under 8-2-2, control transfers at the estimated boundary crossing or an agreed time/point. The transferring unit must forward changed flight plan or control data. The accepting unit must notify if unable to accept under the specified terms, specifying the conditions required. The accepting unit must not alter the clearance before the transfer point without prior approval. Where nonradar separation minima are applied, communications transfer must be made 5 minutes before the boundary unless otherwise agreed.

choices.text: Notify the transferring unit of the missing amendment, request the data, and arrange the communications transfer immediately since the 5-minute lead time has passed.
Accept the aircraft without the amendment and change the clearance after the transfer point without telling the transferring unit.
Wait for the aircraft to check in on the boundary before requesting any missing data.
Transfer communications immediately and tell the pilot to contact the transferring unit for the amendment.

content.explanation: Per 8-2-2, the accepting unit must notify the transferring unit of conditions needed for acceptance. The communications transfer should have occurred at the 5-minute mark for nonradar operations.

content.situation: You are applying 10-minute longitudinal separation. An automated position report update shows the trailing aircraft is gaining and the actual spacing will drop below 10 minutes before the next fix.
content.para_context: Under 8-3-1, longitudinal separation must ensure estimated positions never drop below the prescribed minimum. If an updated control estimate shows the minimum no longer exists, controllers must reestablish separation and issue traffic information as necessary.

choices.text: Wait for the next pilot position report before taking any action.
Continue applying 10 minutes because the original estimate was valid when separation was established.
Take action to reestablish separation and issue traffic information as necessary.
Switch to distance-based separation from Chapter 6 without issuing traffic information.

content.explanation: Per 8-3-1d, the controller must reestablish separation and issue traffic information when an updated estimate shows the minimum no longer exists.

content.para_context: Vertical separation may be discontinued after both aircraft have reported passing ground-based NAVAIDs or DME fixes indicating that they have passed each other, or after both have reported passing a significant point and have at least the applicable same-direction longitudinal spacing.

Discontinue vertical separation immediately after estimated passing time regardless of reports.

Vertical separation may be discontinued because both aircraft have reported passing NAVAID fixes indicating they have passed each other.

content.situation: You want to apply Mach number technique to two turbojet aircraft. Both report over a common point. The lead aircraft is assigned Mach.84, the following aircraft is assigned Mach.82. Both are on the same track and in level flight.
content.para_context: Under 8-3-3, Mach number technique may be applied when the preceding aircraft maintains a Mach number equal to or greater than the following aircraft (minimum 10 minutes), or when the preceding aircraft is faster by specific Mach differentials (5-9 minutes depending on the difference).

choices.text: Apply 12-minute minimum because the lead aircraft is faster by 0.02.
Apply 8-minute minimum because the Mach difference is 0.02.
Apply 10-minute minimum longitudinal separation because the preceding aircraft maintains Mach.84 which is greater than Mach.82, so 10 minutes applies.
Mach number technique cannot be applied because both aircraft must be at exactly the same Mach number.

content.explanation: Per 8-3-3e1(a), when the preceding aircraft maintains a Mach number equal to or greater than the following, the minimum is 10 minutes. Note that the 5-9 minute minima require the preceding aircraft to be faster by at least 0.02-0.06 Mach, but 8-3-3e2's table is a different rule — the 10 minute rule in e1 applies when preceding is equal or greater.

content.situation: A NAVAID flight inspection aircraft is conducting a scheduled inspection run in your sector. You have known traffic at a converging altitude, but the flight inspection pilot has not yet reported his intentions for the next segment of the inspection. The pilot has not agreed to relay-only coordination.
content.question_text: What coordination is required with the flight inspection pilot?

choices.text: Maintain direct contact and exchange information regarding the known traffic and the pilot's intentions.
Issue traffic to the other aircraft only and let the flight inspection aircraft continue uninterrupted without any contact.
Ask the other aircraft to avoid the area and wait for the flight inspection pilot to call you with intentions.
Vector the flight inspection aircraft off its planned route to separate it from the known traffic immediately.

content.explanation: Per 9-1-1, unless otherwise agreed to, maintain direct contact with the pilot and exchange information regarding known traffic in the area and the pilot's intentions. The controller must proactively share known traffic information and solicit the pilot's intentions.

content.situation: You are working a Flight Check aircraft that has reported 'recorded run' on final approach. Another aircraft requests to cross the approach path at a point that would require the Flight Check aircraft to execute a missed approach early. No emergency exists.
content.question_text: How should you handle the crossing traffic request?

choices.text: Suggest flight path adjustments to the other aircraft to avoid penetrating the area where the flight inspection function is being performed.
Clear the other aircraft across the approach path and accept that the recorded run will need to be reinitiated.
Ask the Flight Check aircraft to terminate the recorded run early to accommodate the crossing traffic.
Alternate the crossing traffic with the Flight Check aircraft using half the normal separation minimums to expedite both.

content.explanation: Per 9-1-2c, suggest flight path adjustments for any aircraft that will enter or penetrate an area where a flight inspection function is being performed. Interrupting a recorded run requires the entire procedure to be reinitiated, so protecting the run serves both operational efficiency and the flight inspection mission.
