content.situation: A flight check aircraft filed a specific route and altitude in its initial flight plan for a NAVAID inspection. Another controller suggests assigning a more direct routing and a higher altitude to help the aircraft save fuel and avoid a weather buildup, but the pilot has not requested any change.
content.question_text: How should you handle the routing and altitude for this aircraft?

choices.text: Avoid changes in the route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan.
Assign the more direct routing and higher altitude because it benefits the pilot and saves fuel.
Ask the pilot whether he prefers the filed route or the suggested direct routing and follow his choice.
Change the altitude to avoid weather but keep the route as filed, since altitude changes are always permitted.

content.explanation: Per 9-1-3b, avoid changes in the route or altitude from that filed by the pilot in the initial flight plan. Even well-intentioned changes (fuel savings, weather avoidance) should not be imposed; the filed plan governs unless the pilot requests a change or an emergency requires deviation.

content.situation: A military aircraft with 'dangerous cargo' in its flight plan remarks needs a route amendment due to weather. The pilot refuses the amendment. The other controller wants to issue the amendment anyway because sector traffic requires it.
content.question_text: How should you proceed after the pilot refuses the clearance amendment?

choices.text: Accept the refusal, ask if the traffic delay is acceptable or if an alternate route/altitude is desired, and offer all possible assistance.
Issue the amendment anyway because route changes for weather are always mandatory.
Hold the aircraft without further discussion because refusal of a clearance amendment is not permitted.
Terminate radar services and instruct the pilot to contact FSS for alternate routing.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-1c, when the pilot refuses a clearance amending route/altitude, the pilot will advise if the traffic delay is acceptable or if an alternate route/altitude is desired. ATC must offer all possible assistance.

content.situation: You are providing security tracking to a VFR aircraft in the DC SFRA. The aircraft is not landing at an airport within the SFRA and is approaching the SFRA boundary. The aircraft has been on an assigned transponder code.
content.question_text: How do you terminate security services for this aircraft?

choices.text: Provide security services until the aircraft exits the area, then advise squawk VFR and frequency change approved.
Terminate services at the boundary and tell the aircraft to squawk standby.
Instruct the aircraft to remain on the assigned transponder code until after landing at its destination outside the SFRA.
Hand off to the next sector without any special termination instruction.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-10c1, if not landing within the area, provide security services until the aircraft exits, then advise squawk VFR and frequency change approved. The 'remain on assigned code until landing' instruction (9-2-10c2) applies only to aircraft landing inside the area.
content.para_context: ATC SECURITY SERVICES FOR THE WASHINGTON, DC, SPECIAL FLIGHT RULES

content.question_text: A trainee says: 'We don't know anything about the SECNOT aircraft yet, so I'll wait until we have radar contact to notify the OS/CIC.' Is this advice correct?

choices.text: Correct. OS/CIC notification should wait until the aircraft is positively identified on radar.
Correct, but only if the aircraft is outside controlled airspace.
Incorrect. Forward known information to the OS/CIC and, when information is not known, broadcast the call sign on all frequencies.
Incorrect, but the only required action is to notify adjacent sectors.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-11, the controller must forward all known information to the OS/CIC. When information is not known, broadcast the call sign on all frequencies and advise the OS/CIC of the response. Waiting for radar contact is not an authorized approach.

content.question_text: A law enforcement aircraft requests assistance in locating a suspect aircraft. A controller is concerned about workload. Under 9-2-12, what must be provided?

choices.text: Assist in locating the suspect aircraft when requested, while ensuring assistance does not compromise separation minima or safety.
Deny the request because locating suspect aircraft is not an ATC function.
Provide assistance only if the law enforcement aircraft declares an emergency.
Transfer the request to the DEN and take no further action.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-12a3, when requested, assist law enforcement in locating suspect aircraft. However, 9-2-12a2 limits this: assistance must not compromise approved separation minima or place aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.

content.situation: Aerial refueling is in progress. MARSA has been declared between the tanker and receiver. The tanker has not yet completed rendezvous with the receiver. Another controller suggests issuing a heading change to the tanker to avoid a weather cell.
content.question_text: How should the heading change request be handled?

choices.text: Avoid issuing course or altitude changes prior to rendezvous, because such changes will automatically void MARSA.
Issue the heading change immediately because weather avoidance takes priority over MARSA.
Issue the heading change but tell the tanker that MARSA remains in effect.
Ask the receiver to accept the heading change instead, since receiver course changes do not void MARSA.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-13d and its note, after MARSA has been declared, avoid issuing course or altitude changes prior to rendezvous, because altitude or course changes issued will automatically void MARSA.

content.situation: A pilot's filed route passes through a forecast hazardous radiation area. The pilot has not yet requested a reroute. Another controller says the pilot doesn't need to be told because the radiation hazard may dissipate before the aircraft arrives.
content.question_text: What action must you take regarding the radiation area?

choices.text: Advise the pilot that the proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous radiation area and reroute if the pilot requests it.
Automatically reroute the aircraft without advising the pilot to save time.
Wait to see if the hazard dissipates and advise only if it persists within 50 NM of the aircraft.
Take no action because radiation hazards are the pilot's responsibility under 14 CFR part 91.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-16a, advise pilots whenever their proposed flight path will traverse a reported or forecast hazardous radiation area and reroute when requested. The advice is mandatory regardless of whether the pilot might already know.

content.situation: A SAMP call sign aircraft requests an altitude change and a route deviation to conduct aerial sampling. Your sector has several IFR aircraft on conflicting routes. Another controller suggests denying the SAMP request to avoid reclearing other traffic.
content.question_text: How should the SAMP aircraft request be handled?

choices.text: Approve the SAMP requests to the maximum extent possible; other IFR aircraft may be recleared to accommodate the SAMP mission.
Deny the request because SAMP flights have no priority over other IFR traffic.
Approve only the altitude change and deny the route deviation because dual requests cannot both be approved.

content.situation: A nonstandard cell formation of four military aircraft is approaching its planned breakup fix in your sector. The formation's flight plan shows destinations beyond the breakup point at four separate airfields.
content.question_text: What is the correct clearance limit for this formation?

choices.text: Clear each aircraft individually to its destination before the breakup occurs.
Clear to the ARTCC boundary and let the next center handle the breakup.
Clear the formation to the breakup fix as the clearance limit.
Clear to the first fix beyond the breakup point to minimize frequency changes.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-21e, clear to the breakup fix as the clearance limit. Route and altitude data beyond the breakup point is forwarded as routine flight plan information to the center serving the destination. This keeps the formation clearance clean while ensuring downstream facilities have needed data.

content.situation: An experimental aircraft has been cleared for a specific test profile that was approved according to pilot request. The test profile involves a climb to an unusually high altitude followed by a rapid descent. Traffic picks up and another controller suggests asking the pilot to level off early and delay the descent. No emergency exists.
content.question_text: How should you handle the request to deviate from the approved test profile?

choices.text: Do not ask the pilot to deviate from the planned action; the profile was already approved and only an emergency would justify deviation.
Ask the pilot to level off early because traffic volume creates a practical need to modify the profile.
Cancel the test profile clearance entirely and re-clear the aircraft under standard IFR procedures.
Ask the pilot to hold at the current altitude until all other traffic has passed through the sector.

content.explanation: Per 9-2-3a2, once approved, do not ask the pilot to deviate from a planned action except to preclude an emergency situation. Traffic volume is not an emergency that justifies deviation.

Ignore the report unless the supervisor specifically directs you to monitor the area.

Treat it as routine traffic and take no special action.

content.situation: An active Restricted Area has an upper limit of FL 230. A nonparticipating IFR aircraft at FL 220 requests to continue on its present course, which will take it over the Restricted Area.
content.question_text: What altitude does the nonparticipating aircraft need to maintain for overflight?

choices.text: At least 500 feet above the upper limit — FL 235 or higher.
FL 240 is acceptable because any altitude above the area's upper limit is sufficient.
The aircraft must be at least 2,000 feet above the upper limit — FL 250 or higher.
Lateral separation of 3 miles is the only option; overflight is not permitted.

content.explanation: Per 9-3-2a, nonparticipating aircraft must be assigned at least 500 feet above the upper limit of the airspace. With an upper limit of FL 230, the aircraft needs FL 235 or higher. Above FL 290, the buffer increases to 1,000 feet.

content.question_text: May you request the aircraft to fly a different route?

choices.text: Yes, because 9-4-2 allows requesting a different route for non-emergency fuel dumps in either VFR or IFR conditions.

No, because any fuel-dumping operation is treated as an emergency under 9-4-2.
Yes, but only after the dump is complete and the aircraft is below 10,000 feet.

content.explanation: Per 9-4-2, except when dumping for emergency reasons, an aircraft in either VFR or IFR conditions may be requested to fly a different route.

content.situation: An IFR aircraft is dumping fuel. The highest obstacle within 5 miles of the dump pattern is a peak at 4,500 feet MSL. A trainee assigns the aircraft 5,500 feet.
content.question_text: Is the trainee's altitude assignment of 5,500 feet correct?

choices.text: Yes. 5,500 feet provides standard 1,000-foot IFR obstacle clearance.
Yes. 5,500 feet is 1,000 feet above the peak, and 9-4-3 requires only 1,000 feet.
No. 9-4-3 requires exactly 2,000 feet MSL above ground level, not above the obstacle.
No. The minimum altitude is 6,500 feet (4,500 + 2,000).

content.explanation: Per 9-4-3, IFR fuel-dumping aircraft must be assigned at least 2,000 feet above the highest obstacle within 5 miles of the route or pattern. The formula is 4,500 + 2,000 = 6,500 feet MSL minimum. The trainee's 5,500 feet is 1,000 feet short.

content.situation: A trainee tells you: 'A moored balloon slipped its moorings near the airport, so I'm going to flight follow it and provide separation like we do for unmanned free balloons.'
content.question_text: Is the trainee's response correct?

choices.text: Yes. All derelict balloons require flight following and separation regardless of type.
No. Moored balloons that slip moorings are handled under 9-6-1, not 9-6-2.
No. A derelict moored balloon requires only traffic advisories per 9-6-2.
Yes, but the trainee should also broadcast advisories at 3-minute intervals.

content.explanation: Per 9-6-2, derelict moored balloons require only traffic advisories. Flight following and separation apply to derelict unmanned free balloons. The trainee is applying the unmanned-free-balloon procedures to a moored-balloon situation — these are different response categories.

content.situation: A parachute operation is requested in Class C airspace. The jump operator wants to use an area that is within the Class C boundary but was not previously designated for jumping. A trainee says you can authorize it anywhere in Class C because the airspace is controlled.
content.question_text: May you authorize the jump in undesignated airspace within Class C?

choices.text: No. 9-7-2a requires authorizing parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity, not anywhere within Class C.
Yes. Any airspace within the Class C boundary qualifies as designated for jumping activity once the tower approves it.
Yes. If the jump aircraft can maintain radio contact, the entire Class C surface area is considered designated.
No. Class C airspace may never be used for parachute operations under any circumstances.

content.explanation: Per 9-7-2a, authorize parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity. Class C boundaries are not the same as designated jump airspace.

content.situation: A jump aircraft calls you 2 minutes before the planned drop time in Class E airspace. The pilot asks for authorization to begin jumping. You have three nonparticipating aircraft that will transit the jump airspace during the operation.
content.question_text: What issues exist with this approach?

choices.text: There are no issues. Two minutes is sufficient for Class E jumps under any conditions.
Only the 5-minute rule is an issue; advisories to nonparticipating aircraft are optional in Class E.
Radio contact must be established at least 5 minutes before jumping begins to receive traffic information.
Neither the 5-minute rule nor nonparticipating advisories apply — those are only for Class D operations.

content.explanation: Per 14 CFR 105.13, radio contact must be established at least 5 minutes before jumping begins to receive traffic information. Additionally, per 9-7-4, advisories to nonparticipating aircraft must include location, time, duration, and altitude from which the jump will be made.

operations.problem: Enforce a self-contained activity prompt and answer ordering that does not depend on source-object position.
operations.source_basis: The scenario, controlling rule, and answer set are unchanged.

content.situation: A pilot reports fast-moving lights at an unusual altitude with no corresponding radar return. A trainee says to log it and mention it at the end of the shift because it doesn't affect separation and may just be a weather balloon.
content.question_text: What does 9-8-1 require in this situation?

choices.text: Inform the operations supervisor/CIC of the reported UAP/unexplained phenomena activity immediately.
Wait until the end of the shift to file a report, because no separation was affected.
Ignore the report unless the pilot can provide photographic evidence of the sighting.
Broadcast an advisory to all aircraft on the frequency before informing supervision.

content.explanation: Per 9-8-1, any reported or observed UAP or unexplained phenomena activity must be reported to the operations supervisor/CIC. There is no requirement to wait until end of shift or for independent confirmation.
